title: Georgetown University

infobox.value: * [CUWMA](/wiki/Consortium_of_Universities_of_the_Washington_Metropolitan_Area "Consortium of Universities of the Washington Metropolitan Area") * [COFHE](/wiki/Consortium_on_Financing_Higher_Education "Consortium on Financing Higher Education") * [CUMU](/wiki/Coalition_of_Urban_and_Metropolitan_Universities "Coalition of Urban and Metropolitan Universities") * [GULF](/wiki/Global_University_Leaders_Forum "Global University Leaders Forum")

* [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") – [Big East](/wiki/Big_East_Conference "Big East Conference") * [Patriot League](/wiki/Patriot_League "Patriot League") * [EARC](/wiki/Eastern_Association_of_Rowing_Colleges "Eastern Association of Rowing Colleges") * [EAWRC](/wiki/Eastern_Association_of_Women%27s_Rowing_Colleges "Eastern Association of Women's Rowing Colleges")

is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), United States. Founded by Bishop [John Carroll](/wiki/John_Carroll_(archbishop_of_Baltimore) "John Carroll (archbishop of Baltimore)") in 1789,[[c]](#cite_note-13) it is the oldest [Catholic institution of higher education](/wiki/Catholic_higher_education "Catholic higher education") in the United States, the oldest university in Washington, D.C.,[[d]](#cite_note-14) and the nation's first [federally chartered](/wiki/University_charter#Federal "University charter") university.

**Georgetown University** is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), United States. Founded by Bishop [John Carroll](/wiki/John_Carroll_(archbishop_of_Baltimore) "John Carroll (archbishop of Baltimore)") in 1789,[[c]](#cite_note-13) it is the oldest [Catholic institution of higher education](/wiki/Catholic_higher_education "Catholic higher education") in the United States, the oldest university in Washington, D.C.,[[d]](#cite_note-14) and the nation's first [federally chartered](/wiki/University_charter#Federal "University charter") university.

The university has eleven [undergraduate](/wiki/Undergraduate_education "Undergraduate education") and [graduate](/wiki/Postgraduate_education "Postgraduate education") schools. Its main campus, located in the [Georgetown](/wiki/Georgetown_(Washington,_D.C.) "Georgetown (Washington, D.C.)") historic neighborhood, is on a hill above the [Potomac River](/wiki/Potomac_River "Potomac River") and identifiable by [Healy Hall](/wiki/Healy_Hall "Healy Hall"), a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark"). It is [classified](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") among ["R1: Doctoral Universities – Very high research activity."](/wiki/List_of_research_universities_in_the_United_States#Universities_classified_as_"R1:_Doctoral_Universities_–_Very_high_research_activity" "List of research universities in the United States") The university offers degree programs in forty-eight disciplines, enrolling an average of 7,500 undergraduate and 10,000 graduate students from more than 135 countries. The school's athletic teams are nicknamed the [Hoyas](/wiki/Georgetown_Hoyas "Georgetown Hoyas") and include a [men's basketball team](/wiki/Georgetown_Hoyas_men%27s_basketball "Georgetown Hoyas men's basketball"), which is a member of the [Big East Conference](/wiki/Big_East_Conference "Big East Conference").

[Notable alumni](/wiki/List_of_Georgetown_University_alumni "List of Georgetown University alumni") include 32 [Rhodes Scholars](/wiki/Rhodes_Scholars "Rhodes Scholars"), 46 [Marshall Scholars](/wiki/Marshall_Scholarship "Marshall Scholarship"), 33 [Truman Scholars](/wiki/Truman_Scholars "Truman Scholars"), 565 [Fulbright Scholars](/wiki/Fulbright_Program "Fulbright Program"), at least 10 living [billionaires](/wiki/List_of_universities_by_number_of_billionaire_alumni "List of universities by number of billionaire alumni"), 26 [U.S. governors](/wiki/List_of_current_United_States_governors "List of current United States governors"), 2 [U.S. Supreme Court justices](/wiki/List_of_Justices_of_the_Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "List of Justices of the Supreme Court of the United States"), 2 [U.S. presidents](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"), and 116 members of the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") including 26 [senators](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate"), as well as international royalty and more than a dozen foreign [heads of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state"). Georgetown has educated more [U.S. diplomats](/wiki/United_States_Foreign_Service "United States Foreign Service") than any other university including at least 92 [ambassadors of the United States](/wiki/Ambassadors_of_the_United_States "Ambassadors of the United States"), as well as a number of American politicians and [civil servants](/wiki/Civil_service "Civil service").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Georgetown University](/wiki/History_of_Georgetown_University "History of Georgetown University")

See also: [List of presidents of Georgetown University](/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_Georgetown_University "List of presidents of Georgetown University")

### Founding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Founding")]

[![A painting of an elderly Carroll seated wearing a long gray robe.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/John_Carroll_Gilbert_Stuart.jpg/250px-John_Carroll_Gilbert_Stuart.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Carroll_Gilbert_Stuart.jpg)

[John Carroll](/wiki/John_Carroll_(archbishop_of_Baltimore) "John Carroll (archbishop of Baltimore)"), the first [Archbishop of Baltimore](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Baltimore "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Baltimore") and founder of Georgetown University in 1789

In 1634, [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") settlers from England founded the [Province of Maryland](/wiki/Province_of_Maryland "Province of Maryland") in [colonial-era](/wiki/Colonial_history_of_the_United_States "Colonial history of the United States") [British America](/wiki/British_America "British America").[[11]](#cite_note-jstor-15) In 1646, the defeat of the [Royalists](/wiki/Cavalier "Cavalier") in the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War") led to stringent laws against [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") education and the extradition of known Jesuits from the colony, including missionary [Andrew White](/wiki/Andrew_White_(missionary) "Andrew White (missionary)"), and the destruction of their school at Calverton Manor.[[5]](#cite_note-Nevils-7) During most of the remainder of Maryland's colonial period, Jesuits conducted Catholic schools clandestinely. Following the end of the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), plans to establish a permanent Catholic institution for education in the United States were realized.[[12]](#cite_note-ce-16)

At [Benjamin Franklin](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin "Benjamin Franklin")'s recommendation, [Pope Pius VI](/wiki/Pope_Pius_VI "Pope Pius VI") appointed former Jesuit [John Carroll](/wiki/John_Carroll_(archbishop_of_Baltimore) "John Carroll (archbishop of Baltimore)") the first [head of the Catholic Church in the United States](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Baltimore "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Baltimore"), even though the papal [suppression of the Jesuit order](/wiki/Suppression_of_the_Society_of_Jesus "Suppression of the Society of Jesus") was still in effect. Carroll began meetings of local clergy in 1783 near [Annapolis](/wiki/Annapolis,_Maryland "Annapolis, Maryland"), where they orchestrated the development of a new university.[[13]](#cite_note-ben-17) On January 23, 1789, Carroll finalized the purchase of the property in Georgetown on which Dahlgren Quadrangle was later built.[[14]](#cite_note-loyola-18) Future Congressman [William Gaston](/wiki/William_Gaston "William Gaston") was enrolled as the school's first student on November 22, 1791, and instruction began on January 2, 1792.[[13]](#cite_note-ben-17)

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 19th century")]

During its early years, Georgetown College suffered from considerable financial strain.[[15]](#cite_note-19) The Maryland Society of Jesus began its restoration in 1805, and Jesuit affiliation, in the form of teachers and administrators, bolstered confidence in the college.[[16]](#cite_note-bulletin-20) The school relied on private sources of funding and the limited profits from local lands which had been donated to the Jesuits. To raise money for Georgetown and other schools in 1838, Maryland Jesuits conducted a [mass sale](/wiki/1838_Jesuit_slave_sale "1838 Jesuit slave sale") of some 272 slaves to two [Deep South](/wiki/Deep_South "Deep South") plantations in [Maringouin, Louisiana](/wiki/Maringouin,_Louisiana "Maringouin, Louisiana"), from their six in Maryland, ending their slaveholding.[[17]](#cite_note-21)[[18]](#cite_note-22)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Georgetown_University_c._1850.jpg/250px-Georgetown_University_c._1850.jpg)](/wiki/File:Georgetown_University_c._1850.jpg)

Georgetown University, c. 1850

President [James Madison](/wiki/James_Madison "James Madison") signed into law Georgetown's [congressional charter](/wiki/Congressional_charter "Congressional charter") on March 1, 1815, creating the first federal [university charter](/wiki/University_charter "University charter"), which allowed it to confer degrees, with the first bachelor's degrees being awarded two years later.[[19]](#cite_note-charter-23)[[20]](#cite_note-24)

In 1844, the school received a [corporate](/wiki/Corporation "Corporation") charter under the name "[The President and Directors of Georgetown College](/wiki/President_and_Directors_of_Georgetown_College "President and Directors of Georgetown College")", affording the growing school additional legal rights. In response to the demand for a local option for Catholic students, the Medical School was founded in 1851.[[21]](#cite_note-25)

[![Black-and-white photo of several military men idling on a riverbank. Across the river are several large buildings](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Georgetown_1861.jpg/250px-Georgetown_1861.jpg)](/wiki/File:Georgetown_1861.jpg)

[Union army](/wiki/Union_army "Union army") soldiers on [Theodore Roosevelt Island](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt_Island "Theodore Roosevelt Island") with the [Potomac River](/wiki/Potomac_River "Potomac River") and the university visible in the background in 1861 at the beginning of the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War")

[![Black and white photo of an older man wearing black with a priest's colar and facing right.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Patrick_Francis_Healy.jpg/250px-Patrick_Francis_Healy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Patrick_Francis_Healy.jpg)

[Patrick Francis Healy](/wiki/Patrick_Francis_Healy "Patrick Francis Healy"), the first African-American to become a [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit"), helped transform the school into a modern university after the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War")[[22]](#cite_note-26)[[23]](#cite_note-27)

The [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") greatly impacted Georgetown as 1,141 students and alumni enlisted in one army or the other, and the [Union army](/wiki/Union_army "Union army") commandeered university buildings in order to defend the national capital from a feared [Confederate](/wiki/Confederate_States_Army "Confederate States Army") attack.[[12]](#cite_note-ce-16) By the time President [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") visited the Georgetown campus in May 1861, 1,400 troops were living in temporary quarters there. The number of lives lost in the Civil war caused enrollment levels to remain low until well after the war. Only seven students graduated in 1869, down from over 300 in the previous decade.[[24]](#cite_note-28) When the Georgetown College Boat Club, the school's [rowing](/wiki/Rowing_(sport) "Rowing (sport)") team, was founded in 1876 it adopted two colors: blue, used for [Union uniforms](/wiki/Uniform_of_the_Union_Army "Uniform of the Union Army"), and gray, used for [Confederate uniforms](/wiki/Uniforms_of_the_Confederate_States_military_forces "Uniforms of the Confederate States military forces"). These colors signified the peaceful existence of students who held various loyalties.[[25]](#cite_note-29)

Enrollment did not recover until the late 19th century, during the presidency of [Patrick Francis Healy](/wiki/Patrick_Francis_Healy "Patrick Francis Healy") from 1873 to 1881. Born in [Athens, Georgia](/wiki/Athens,_Georgia "Athens, Georgia") as a slave by law and [mixed-race](/wiki/Mixed-race "Mixed-race") by ancestry, Healy was the first person of [African](/wiki/African_American "African American") descent to head a predominantly white American university.[[e]](#cite_note-healy-30) He identified as Irish Catholic, like his father, and was educated in Catholic schools in the United States and France. He is credited with reforming the undergraduate [curriculum](/wiki/Curriculum "Curriculum"), lengthening the medical and law programs, and creating the [Alumni Association](/wiki/Alumni_association "Alumni association"). One of his largest undertakings was the construction of a major new building, subsequently named [Healy Hall](/wiki/Healy_Hall "Healy Hall") in his honor. For his work, Healy is known as the school's "second founder".[[26]](#cite_note-pfh-31)

In 1870, after the founding of the [Law Department](/wiki/Georgetown_University_Law_Center "Georgetown University Law Center"), Healy and his successors hoped to bind the professional schools into a university, and focus on [higher education](/wiki/Higher_education "Higher education").[[16]](#cite_note-bulletin-20)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 20th century")]

The [School of Medicine](/wiki/Georgetown_University_School_of_Medicine "Georgetown University School of Medicine") added a dental school in 1901, and the undergraduate [School of Nursing](/wiki/Georgetown_University_School_of_Nursing "Georgetown University School of Nursing") was established 1903.[[27]](#cite_note-32) [Georgetown Preparatory School](/wiki/Georgetown_Preparatory_School "Georgetown Preparatory School") relocated from campus in 1919 and fully separated from the university in 1927.[[28]](#cite_note-33)

The [School of Foreign Service](/wiki/Walsh_School_of_Foreign_Service "Walsh School of Foreign Service") (SFS) was founded in 1919 by [Edmund A. Walsh](/wiki/Edmund_A._Walsh "Edmund A. Walsh") to prepare students for leadership in diplomacy and foreign commerce.[[16]](#cite_note-bulletin-20) The [School of Dentistry](/wiki/Georgetown_University_School_of_Dentistry "Georgetown University School of Dentistry") became independent of the School of Medicine in 1956.[[29]](#cite_note-34) The School of Business Administration was separated from the SFS in 1957 and was renamed the [McDonough School of Business](/wiki/McDonough_School_of_Business "McDonough School of Business") (MSB) in 1998 in honor of SFS alumnus Robert E. McDonough.[[30]](#cite_note-35)[[31]](#cite_note-36)

Georgetown also aimed to expand its resources and student body. The School of Nursing has admitted female students since its founding, and most of the university classes were made available to women on a limited basis by 1952.[[32]](#cite_note-37) With the College of Arts and Sciences welcoming its first female students in the 1969–1970 [academic year](/wiki/Academic_year "Academic year"), Georgetown became fully [coeducational](/wiki/Coeducation "Coeducation").[[33]](#cite_note-38)

In 1962, the [Center for Strategic and International Studies](/wiki/Center_for_Strategic_and_International_Studies "Center for Strategic and International Studies") (CSIS) was founded at Georgetown University as a [think tank](/wiki/Think_tank "Think tank") to conduct policy studies and strategic analyses of political, economic and security issues throughout the world.[[34]](#cite_note-39) When [Henry Kissinger](/wiki/Henry_Kissinger "Henry Kissinger") retired from his position as [U.S. Secretary of State](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_State "United States Secretary of State") in 1977, he taught at Georgetown SFS, making CSIS the base for his Washington operations.[[35]](#cite_note-40)[[36]](#cite_note-Kissinger-41)[[37]](#cite_note-42) In 1986, the university's [board of directors](/wiki/Board_of_directors "Board of directors") voted to sever all ties with CSIS due to differences in academic direction and competing fund-raising efforts.[[38]](#cite_note-sever-43)

[![A large Gothic-style stone building dominated by a tall clocktower.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Georgetown_University_%2853821005319%29.jpg/250px-Georgetown_University_%2853821005319%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Georgetown_University_(53821005319).jpg)

[Healy Hall](/wiki/Healy_Hall "Healy Hall"), which houses classrooms and the university's executive body

In 1975, Georgetown established the [Center for Contemporary Arab Studies](/wiki/Center_for_Contemporary_Arab_Studies "Center for Contemporary Arab Studies"), soliciting funds from the governments of the United States, [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia"), [Oman](/wiki/Oman "Oman"), and [Libya](/wiki/Libya "Libya") as well as American corporations with business interests in the Middle East.[[39]](#cite_note-44)[[40]](#cite_note-45) It later returned the money it received from [Muammar Qaddafi](/wiki/Muammar_Gaddafi "Muammar Gaddafi")'s Libyan government, which had been used to fund a chair for [Hisham Shirabi](/wiki/Hisham_Sharabi "Hisham Sharabi"), and also returned further donations from [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq").[[41]](#cite_note-46) Georgetown ended its bicentennial year of 1989 by electing [Leo J. O'Donovan](/wiki/Leo_J._O%27Donovan "Leo J. O'Donovan"), S.J. as president. He subsequently launched the Third Century Campaign to expand the school's endowment.[[42]](#cite_note-degioia-47)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 21st century")]

In December 2003, Georgetown completed the campaign after raising over $1 billion for financial aid, academic chair endowment, and new capital projects.[[43]](#cite_note-48)

In October 2002, Georgetown University began studying the feasibility of opening a campus of the SFS in Qatar, when the non-profit [Qatar Foundation](/wiki/Qatar_Foundation "Qatar Foundation") first proposed the idea. The [School of Foreign Service in Qatar](/wiki/Georgetown_University_School_of_Foreign_Service_in_Qatar "Georgetown University School of Foreign Service in Qatar") opened in 2005 along with four other U.S. universities in the [Education City](/wiki/Education_City,_Qatar "Education City, Qatar") development. Additionally, the [Center for International and Regional Studies](/wiki/Center_for_International_and_Regional_Studies "Center for International and Regional Studies") (CIRS) opened in 2005 at the new Qatar campus.[[44]](#cite_note-49) Between 2012 and 2018, Georgetown received more than $350 million from [Gulf Cooperation Council](/wiki/Gulf_Cooperation_Council "Gulf Cooperation Council") countries including [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia"), [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar "Qatar"), and the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates").[[45]](#cite_note-50)

In 2005, Georgetown received a $20 million gift from [Alwaleed bin Talal bin Abdulaziz Alsaud](/wiki/Al_Waleed_bin_Talal_Al_Saud "Al Waleed bin Talal Al Saud"), a member of the [Saudi Royal Family](/wiki/House_of_Saud "House of Saud"); at that time the second-largest donation ever to the university, it was used to expand the activities of the [Prince Alwaleed Bin Talal Center for Muslim-Christian Understanding](/wiki/Prince_Alwaleed_Bin_Talal_Center_for_Muslim-Christian_Understanding "Prince Alwaleed Bin Talal Center for Muslim-Christian Understanding").[[46]](#cite_note-51) The same year, Georgetown began hosting a two-week workshop at [Fudan University](/wiki/Fudan_University "Fudan University")'s School of International Relations and Public Affairs in [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"), China, which developed into a more formal connection when Georgetown opened a liaison office at Fudan on January 12, 2008, to further collaboration.[[47]](#cite_note-52)

[John J. DeGioia](/wiki/John_J._DeGioia "John J. DeGioia"), Georgetown's first lay president, led the school from 2001 to 2024. DeGioia continued its financial modernization and sought to "expand opportunities for intercultural and interreligious dialogue."[[48]](#cite_note-53) DeGioia also founded the annual Building Bridges Seminar in 2001, which brings global religious leaders together, and is part of Georgetown's effort to promote religious pluralism.[[49]](#cite_note-ir-54) The [Berkley Center for Religion, Peace, and World Affairs](/wiki/Berkley_Center_for_Religion,_Peace,_and_World_Affairs "Berkley Center for Religion, Peace, and World Affairs") began as an initiative in 2004, and after a grant from [William R. Berkley](/wiki/W._R._Berkley "W. R. Berkley"), was launched as an independent organization in 2006.[[49]](#cite_note-ir-54)

### Jesuit tradition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Jesuit tradition")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Dahlgren_Chapel_of_the_Sacred_Heart_Georgetown_University.jpg/250px-Dahlgren_Chapel_of_the_Sacred_Heart_Georgetown_University.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dahlgren_Chapel_of_the_Sacred_Heart_Georgetown_University.jpg)

[Dahlgren Chapel](/wiki/Dahlgren_Chapel_of_the_Sacred_Heart "Dahlgren Chapel of the Sacred Heart") on the university campus

Georgetown University was founded by former [Jesuits](/wiki/Jesuits "Jesuits") in the tradition of [Ignatius of Loyola](/wiki/Ignatius_of_Loyola "Ignatius of Loyola"); it is a member of the [Association of Jesuit Colleges and Universities](/wiki/Association_of_Jesuit_Colleges_and_Universities "Association of Jesuit Colleges and Universities").[[14]](#cite_note-loyola-18)[[50]](#cite_note-55) Georgetown is not a [pontifical university](/wiki/Pontifical_university "Pontifical university"), though seven Jesuits serve on the 36 member Board of Directors, the university's governing body.[[51]](#cite_note-56)

[Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") spaces at the university, including [Dahlgren Chapel](/wiki/Dahlgren_Chapel_of_the_Sacred_Heart "Dahlgren Chapel of the Sacred Heart"), the university's principal place of Catholic worship, fall within the territorial jurisdiction of the [Archdiocese of Washington](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Washington "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Washington"),[[52]](#cite_note-57)

Fifty-two members of the Society of Jesus live on campus, and are employed by Georgetown mostly as professors or administrators.[[53]](#cite_note-58) Jesuit Heritage Week has been held every year since 2001 to celebrate the contributions of Jesuits to the Georgetown tradition.[[54]](#cite_note-59)

Georgetown's Catholic heritage has been controversial at times, even though its influence is relatively limited.[[55]](#cite_note-wildes-60) Stores in university-owned buildings are prohibited from selling or distributing [birth control](/wiki/Birth_control "Birth control") products.[[56]](#cite_note-61)

The university hosts the [Cardinal O'Connor Conference on Life](/wiki/Cardinal_O%27Connor_Conference_on_Life "Cardinal O'Connor Conference on Life") annually in January to discuss the [pro-life](/wiki/Anti-abortion_movements "Anti-abortion movements") movement.[[57]](#cite_note-62) [Georgetown University Medical Center](/wiki/Georgetown_University_Medical_Center "Georgetown University Medical Center") and [Georgetown University Hospital](/wiki/MedStar_Georgetown_University_Hospital "MedStar Georgetown University Hospital"), operated by [MedStar Health](/wiki/MedStar_Health "MedStar Health"), are prohibited from performing abortions.[[58]](#cite_note-medstar-63) However, as of 2004[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit), the hospital was performing research using [embryonic stem cells](/wiki/Embryonic_stem_cells "Embryonic stem cells").[[59]](#cite_note-64)

Between 1996 and 1999, the administration added [crucifixes](/wiki/Crucifix "Crucifix") to many classroom walls, a change that attracted national attention.[[60]](#cite_note-65) Before 1996, crucifixes had hung only in hospital rooms and historic classrooms.[[61]](#cite_note-66) Some of these crucifixes are historic works of art, and are noted as such.[[62]](#cite_note-67)

In May 2004, Imam [Yahya Hendi](/wiki/Yahya_Hendi "Yahya Hendi"), the school's on-campus Muslim cleric, faced pressure to remove crucifixes while he and other campus faith leaders defended their placement.[[63]](#cite_note-68) The [Edward B. Bunn, S.J. Intercultural Center](/wiki/Edward_B._Bunn,_S.J._Intercultural_Center "Edward B. Bunn, S.J. Intercultural Center") rotates displays of various faith and culture symbols in its lobby.[[64]](#cite_note-69)

In September 2005, Georgetown was criticized by religious groups, including the [Cardinal Newman Society](/wiki/Cardinal_Newman_Society "Cardinal Newman Society"), for not following the teachings of the [church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") and hosting [pro-abortion rights](/wiki/Abortion-rights_movements "Abortion-rights movements") speakers, including [John Kerry](/wiki/John_Kerry "John Kerry") and [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama").[[65]](#cite_note-70)[[66]](#cite_note-71)

In 2009, Georgetown's religious symbols were brought back to national attention after the university administration covered-up the [name of Jesus](/wiki/Christogram "Christogram") in preparation for then President Barack Obama's speech on campus.[[67]](#cite_note-72)

In May 2012, [Washington's Archbishop](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Washington "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Washington") [Donald Wuerl](/wiki/Donald_Wuerl "Donald Wuerl") criticized the university for inviting pro-abortion rights [Kathleen Sebelius](/wiki/Kathleen_Sebelius "Kathleen Sebelius") to be a commencement speaker.[[68]](#cite_note-73)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Georgetown_neighborhood_09.jpeg/250px-Georgetown_neighborhood_09.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Georgetown_neighborhood_09.jpeg)

Georgetown neighborhood

In August 2013, religious groups denounced Georgetown for allowing gay-themed events, including a performance, during which "a male student went as a high-heeled [Mary](/wiki/Mary,_mother_of_Jesus "Mary, mother of Jesus") and danced to [Madonna](/wiki/Madonna "Madonna")'s "[Like a Virgin](/wiki/Like_a_Virgin_(song) "Like a Virgin (song)")" while [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") (a woman) looked on."[[69]](#cite_note-74)

As of 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit), the university had 7,463 undergraduate students and 11,542 graduate students.[[76]](#cite_note-factsheet2017-83) [Bachelor's](/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degree "Bachelor's degree") programs are offered through [College of Arts & Sciences](/wiki/Georgetown_University_College_of_Arts_%26_Sciences "Georgetown University College of Arts & Sciences"), the [School of Nursing](/wiki/Georgetown_University_School_of_Nursing "Georgetown University School of Nursing"), the [McDonough School of Business](/wiki/McDonough_School_of_Business "McDonough School of Business"), the [School of Continuing Studies](/wiki/Georgetown_University_School_of_Continuing_Studies "Georgetown University School of Continuing Studies"), the [School of Health](/wiki/Georgetown_University_School_of_Health "Georgetown University School of Health"), and the [Walsh School of Foreign Service](/wiki/Walsh_School_of_Foreign_Service "Walsh School of Foreign Service"), which includes the [Qatar campus](/wiki/Georgetown_University_in_Qatar "Georgetown University in Qatar").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![Three young adults lie on grass reading books in front of a brick building with many windows](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Georgetown_Jesuit_Residence.jpg/250px-Georgetown_Jesuit_Residence.jpg)](/wiki/File:Georgetown_Jesuit_Residence.jpg)

Students studying outside Wolfington Hall Jesuit Residence

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Georgetown_University_Office_of_Undergraduate_Admissions_%2853820914198%29.jpg/250px-Georgetown_University_Office_of_Undergraduate_Admissions_%2853820914198%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Georgetown_University_Office_of_Undergraduate_Admissions_(53820914198).jpg)

White-Gravenor Hall hosts the Office of Undergraduate Admissions

[Master's](/wiki/Master%27s_degree "Master's degree") and [doctoral](/wiki/Doctor_of_Philosophy "Doctor of Philosophy") programs are offered through the Graduate School of Arts & Sciences, the Law Center, the School of Medicine, the [McCourt School of Public Policy](/wiki/McCourt_School_of_Public_Policy "McCourt School of Public Policy"), and the School of Continuing Studies. Master's students occasionally share some advanced [seminars](/wiki/Seminar "Seminar") with undergraduates, and most undergraduate schools offer abbreviated bachelor's and master's programs following completion of the undergraduate degree. The [McDonough School of Business](/wiki/McDonough_School_of_Business "McDonough School of Business") and the [Edmund A. Walsh School of Foreign Service](/wiki/Edmund_A._Walsh_School_of_Foreign_Service "Edmund A. Walsh School of Foreign Service") both offer master's programs. The School of Foreign Service is renowned for its academic programs in international affairs. The [Center for Contemporary Arab Studies](/wiki/Center_for_Contemporary_Arab_Studies "Center for Contemporary Arab Studies") also offer a Master's of Arab Studies, as well as certificates.[[77]](#cite_note-84)

Each graduate school offers at least one [double degree](/wiki/Double_degree "Double degree") with another graduate school.[[78]](#cite_note-85) Additionally, the Law Center offers a joint degree with the [Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Bloomberg_School_of_Public_Health "Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health").[[79]](#cite_note-86) The School of Continuing Studies includes the Center for Continuing and Professional Education, and operates four types of degree programs, over thirty professional certificates and non-degree courses, undergraduate and [graduate degrees in Liberal Studies](/wiki/Master_of_Arts_in_Liberal_Studies "Master of Arts in Liberal Studies"), as well as summer courses for graduates, undergraduates, and high school students.[[80]](#cite_note-87) The School of Dentistry closed in 1990 after 89 years in operation.[[81]](#cite_note-88)

Admission to Georgetown has been deemed "most selective" by *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")*,[[92]](#cite_note-99) with the university receiving 27,650 applications and admitting 11.7% of those who applied for the Class of 2025.[[93]](#cite_note-100) As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit), Georgetown's graduate schools have acceptance rates of 2.8% to the School of Medicine,[[94]](#cite_note-101) 12.9% to the Law Center,[[95]](#cite_note-102) 25% to the [MSFS](/wiki/Master_of_Science_in_Foreign_Service "Master of Science in Foreign Service"),[[96]](#cite_note-103) and 35% to the [MBA program](/wiki/McDonough_School_of_Business "McDonough School of Business").[[97]](#cite_note-GMAT-104) In 2004, a [National Bureau of Economic Research](/wiki/National_Bureau_of_Economic_Research "National Bureau of Economic Research") study on revealed preference of U.S. colleges showed Georgetown was the 16th most-preferred choice.[[98]](#cite_note-105)

The [School of Foreign Service](/wiki/Walsh_School_of_Foreign_Service "Walsh School of Foreign Service")'s (SFS) master's and bachelor's programs in [international relations](/wiki/International_relations "International relations") were ranked first in the world by *[Foreign Policy](/wiki/Foreign_Policy "Foreign Policy")* in 2024.[[99]](#cite_note-106) SFS's undergraduate programs were also ranked first in the United States for [international affairs](/wiki/International_relations "International relations") by [*Niche*](/wiki/Niche_(company) "Niche (company)").[[100]](#cite_note-107) The [McCourt School of Public Policy](/wiki/McCourt_School_of_Public_Policy "McCourt School of Public Policy") is ranked fourth in [global policy](/wiki/International_Politics "International Politics") and [administration studies](/wiki/Global_governance "Global governance") by *[US News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")*.[[101]](#cite_note-108) In the same report, the [Medical School](/wiki/Georgetown_University_School_of_Medicine "Georgetown University School of Medicine") is ranked 44th in research and 87th in primary care,[[102]](#cite_note-109) and the [McDonough School of Business](/wiki/McDonough_School_of_Business "McDonough School of Business") ranks 24th in [MBA programs](/wiki/Master_of_Business_Administration "Master of Business Administration") and 14th in undergraduate programs.[[103]](#cite_note-110) In 2024, *Poets & Quants* ranked Georgetown's undergraduate business programs third in the country.[[104]](#cite_note-111) [Georgetown University Law Center](/wiki/Georgetown_University_Law_Center "Georgetown University Law Center") is ranked 14th in the United States[[105]](#cite_note-:0-112) and 12th in the world,[[106]](#cite_note-113) as well as first in clinical training and part-time law, second in tax law, third in international law, fifth in criminal law, seventh in health care law, ninth in constitutional law, and tenth in environmental law.[[105]](#cite_note-:0-112)

The undergraduate schools maintain a restrictive [Early Action](/wiki/Early_Action "Early Action") admissions program, as students who have applied through an [Early Decision](/wiki/Early_Decision "Early Decision") process at another school are not permitted to apply early to Georgetown.[[76]](#cite_note-factsheet2017-83) 94% of students accepted for the class of 2017 were in the top 10% of their class and the [interquartile range](/wiki/Interquartile_range "Interquartile range") of [SAT](/wiki/SAT "SAT") scores was 700–770 in Reading/Writing and 680–780 in Math.[[107]](#cite_note-114) Georgetown accepts the SAT and [ACT](/wiki/ACT_(test) "ACT (test)"), though it does not consider the writing portion of either test.[[108]](#cite_note-115) Over 55% of undergraduates receive financial aid, and the university meets 100% of demonstrated need, with an average financial aid package of $23,500 and about 70% of aid distributed in the form of grants or scholarships.[[109]](#cite_note-116) Georgetown is [need-blind](/wiki/Need-blind "Need-blind") for domestic applicants.[[110]](#cite_note-117)

### Faculty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Faculty")]

Main article: [List of Georgetown University faculty](/wiki/List_of_Georgetown_University_faculty "List of Georgetown University faculty")

As of 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit), Georgetown University employed 1,414 full-time and 1,196 part-time faculty members across its three Washington, D.C. campuses,[[76]](#cite_note-factsheet2017-83) with additional staff at [Georgetown University in Qatar](/wiki/Georgetown_University_in_Qatar "Georgetown University in Qatar").[[111]](#cite_note-118) The faculty comprises leading academics and notable political and business leaders, and are predominantly male by a two-to-one margin.[[112]](#cite_note-119)

Georgetown University's faculty members give more support to liberal candidates, and while their donation patterns are generally consistent with those of other American university faculties, they gave more than average to [Barack Obama 2008 presidential campaign](/wiki/Barack_Obama_2008_presidential_campaign "Barack Obama 2008 presidential campaign").[[113]](#cite_note-120)[[114]](#cite_note-121)

The faculty includes former [Society for Classical Studies](/wiki/Society_for_Classical_Studies "Society for Classical Studies") president [James J. O'Donnell](/wiki/James_J._O%27Donnell "James J. O'Donnell"), theologian [John Haught](/wiki/John_Haught "John Haught"), social activistd [Chai Feldblum](/wiki/Chai_Feldblum "Chai Feldblum"), [Nobel laureate](/wiki/List_of_Nobel_laureates "List of Nobel laureates") [George Akerlof](/wiki/George_Akerlof "George Akerlof"), writer and human rights advocate [Carolyn Forché](/wiki/Carolyn_Forch%C3%A9 "Carolyn Forché"), award-winning literary critic [Maureen Corrigan](/wiki/Maureen_Corrigan "Maureen Corrigan"), linguist [Deborah Tannen](/wiki/Deborah_Tannen "Deborah Tannen"), business philosopher [Jason Brennan](/wiki/Jason_Brennan "Jason Brennan"), and hip hop scholar [Michael Eric Dyson](/wiki/Michael_Eric_Dyson "Michael Eric Dyson").[[115]](#cite_note-faculty2-122)[[116]](#cite_note-123)[[117]](#cite_note-wsj2-124)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Secretary_Blinken_Meets_with_Georgetown_Faculty_Before_Commencement_Ceremonies_%2852090082673%29.jpg/250px-Secretary_Blinken_Meets_with_Georgetown_Faculty_Before_Commencement_Ceremonies_%2852090082673%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Secretary_Blinken_Meets_with_Georgetown_Faculty_Before_Commencement_Ceremonies_(52090082673).jpg)

[Antony Blinken](/wiki/Antony_Blinken "Antony Blinken"), the [U.S. Secretary of State](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_State "United States Secretary of State"), meets with Georgetown faculty before commencement ceremonies

Many former politicians choose to teach at Georgetown, including former Secretaries of State [Madeleine Albright](/wiki/Madeleine_Albright "Madeleine Albright") and [Henry Kissinger](/wiki/Henry_Kissinger "Henry Kissinger"), former U.S. Ambassador to the United Nations [Jeane Kirkpatrick](/wiki/Jeane_Kirkpatrick "Jeane Kirkpatrick"), [U.S. Agency for International Development](/wiki/United_States_Agency_for_International_Development "United States Agency for International Development") administrator [Andrew Natsios](/wiki/Andrew_Natsios "Andrew Natsios"), National Security Advisor [Anthony Lake](/wiki/Anthony_Lake "Anthony Lake"), and CIA director [George Tenet](/wiki/George_Tenet "George Tenet"). Former [Supreme Court Justices](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") [William J. Brennan, Jr.](/wiki/William_J._Brennan,_Jr. "William J. Brennan, Jr."), [Antonin Scalia](/wiki/Antonin_Scalia "Antonin Scalia"), and [John Roberts](/wiki/John_Roberts "John Roberts") have each taught at the university. Former Solicitor General of the United States [Paul Clement](/wiki/Paul_Clement "Paul Clement") has been a member of the law faculty since leaving public office in 2008.[[118]](#cite_note-:1-125) Internationally, the school attracts former ambassadors and heads of state, including former Prime Minister of Spain [José María Aznar](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Aznar "José María Aznar"), Saudi Ambassador Prince [Turki Al-Faisal](/wiki/Turki_Al-Faisal "Turki Al-Faisal"), [President](/wiki/President_of_Costa_Rica "President of Costa Rica") [Laura Chinchilla](/wiki/Laura_Chinchilla "Laura Chinchilla") of [Costa Rica](/wiki/Costa_Rica "Costa Rica"), and President of [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia") [Álvaro Uribe](/wiki/%C3%81lvaro_Uribe "Álvaro Uribe").[[119]](#cite_note-126)[[120]](#cite_note-127)[[121]](#cite_note-128)

### Research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Research")]

[![A large Georgian-era style building complete in red brick, classical columns and a statue in front](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Georgetown_University_School_of_Medicine_%26_School_of_Dentistry_%2853820913143%29.jpg/250px-Georgetown_University_School_of_Medicine_%26_School_of_Dentistry_%2853820913143%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Georgetown_University_School_of_Medicine_%26_School_of_Dentistry_(53820913143).jpg)

[Georgetown Medical School](/wiki/Georgetown_University_School_of_Medicine "Georgetown University School of Medicine") accounts for a significant portion of the university's research funding, mostly received from the [U.S. government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States")[[122]](#cite_note-129)

Georgetown University is [classified](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") among "R1: Doctoral Universities – Very high research activity".[[123]](#cite_note-130) As of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit), Georgetown's libraries held over 3.5 million printed items, including 1.25 million e-books, in seven buildings, with most in [Lauinger Library](/wiki/Lauinger_Library "Lauinger Library").[[124]](#cite_note-131) The Blommer Science Library in the Reiss Science Building on campus, houses most of the Science collection. Additionally, the Law School campus includes the nation's fifth-largest [law library](/wiki/Georgetown_University_Law_Library "Georgetown University Law Library") as of 2007.[[125]](#cite_note-132) Georgetown faculty conduct research in hundreds of subjects, but research priorities are in religion, ethics, science, public policy, and cancer medicine.[[126]](#cite_note-133)

In 2019, Georgetown spent $240.9 million on research, ranking it 101st nationwide, with $94.0 million in federal funding.[[127]](#cite_note-134) In 2007, it received about $14.8 million in federal funds for research, with 64% from the [National Science Foundation](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation "National Science Foundation"), [National Institutes of Health](/wiki/National_Institutes_of_Health "National Institutes of Health"), the [U.S. Department of Energy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Energy "United States Department of Energy"), and the [U.S. Department of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense").[[128]](#cite_note-sci-135) In 2010, the school received $5.6 million from the [Department of Education](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Education "United States Department of Education") to fund fellowships in several international studies fields.[[129]](#cite_note-136) Georgetown's [Lombardi Comprehensive Cancer Center](/wiki/Georgetown_Lombardi_Comprehensive_Cancer_Center "Georgetown Lombardi Comprehensive Cancer Center") is one of 41 research-intensive comprehensive cancer centers in the United States, and developed the breakthrough [HPV vaccine](/wiki/HPV_vaccine "HPV vaccine") for [cervical cancer](/wiki/Cervical_cancer "Cervical cancer"),[[130]](#cite_note-137) and conditionally reprogrammed cells (CRC) technology.[[131]](#cite_note-lombardi-138)

#### Academic publications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Academic publications")]

Centers that conduct and sponsor research at Georgetown include the [Berkley Center for Religion, Peace, and World Affairs](/wiki/Berkley_Center_for_Religion,_Peace,_and_World_Affairs "Berkley Center for Religion, Peace, and World Affairs"), the [Prince Alwaleed Center for Muslim–Christian Understanding](/wiki/Prince_Alwaleed_Center_for_Muslim%E2%80%93Christian_Understanding "Prince Alwaleed Center for Muslim–Christian Understanding") and the [Woodstock Theological Center](/wiki/Woodstock_Theological_Center "Woodstock Theological Center"). Regular publications include the *[Georgetown Journal on Poverty Law and Policy](/wiki/Georgetown_Journal_on_Poverty_Law_and_Policy "Georgetown Journal on Poverty Law and Policy")*, the *[Kennedy Institute of Ethics Journal](/wiki/Kennedy_Institute_of_Ethics_Journal "Kennedy Institute of Ethics Journal")*, *[The Georgetown Law Journal](/wiki/The_Georgetown_Law_Journal "The Georgetown Law Journal")*, the *[Georgetown Journal of International Affairs](/wiki/Georgetown_Journal_of_International_Affairs "Georgetown Journal of International Affairs")*, and the *[Georgetown Public Policy Review](/wiki/Georgetown_Public_Policy_Review "Georgetown Public Policy Review")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Campuses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Campuses")]

Main article: [Campuses of Georgetown University](/wiki/Campuses_of_Georgetown_University "Campuses of Georgetown University")

See also: [List of Georgetown University buildings](/wiki/List_of_Georgetown_University_buildings "List of Georgetown University buildings")

Georgetown University has four campuses in Washington, D.C.: the undergraduate campus located in the neighborhood of Georgetown, the Medical Center, the School of Continuing Studies (in Chinatown) and the Law Center. The undergraduate campus and Medical Center are together in the [Georgetown neighborhood](/wiki/Georgetown,_Washington,_D.C. "Georgetown, Washington, D.C.") in the [Northwest Quadrant](/wiki/Northwest,_Washington,_D.C. "Northwest, Washington, D.C.") of Washington and form the main campus. Other centers are located around Washington, D.C., including the Center for Continuing and Professional Education at [Clarendon](/wiki/Clarendon,_Arlington,_Virginia "Clarendon, Arlington, Virginia") in [Arlington County, Virginia](/wiki/Arlington_County,_Virginia "Arlington County, Virginia"). Transit between these locations and the [Washington Metro](/wiki/Washington_Metro "Washington Metro") is supplied by a system of shuttles, known as GUTS buses.[[132]](#cite_note-139) Georgetown also has branches of the School of Foreign Service in [Doha](/wiki/Doha "Doha"), Qatar, and [Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta"), Indonesia, as well as villas in [Alanya](/wiki/Alanya "Alanya"), Turkey, and [Fiesole](/wiki/Fiesole "Fiesole"), Italy. In their campus layout, Georgetown's administrators consistently used the traditional [quadrangle](/wiki/Quadrangle_(architecture) "Quadrangle (architecture)") design.[[133]](#cite_note-oldplan-140)

### Main campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Main campus")]

[![A panorama of numerous buildings, particularly the tall clocktower, above a stretch of brightly colored autumn trees all reflected in a river.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Georgetown_Riverview.jpg/250px-Georgetown_Riverview.jpg)](/wiki/File:Georgetown_Riverview.jpg)

Georgetown's campus is built on a rise above the [Potomac River](/wiki/Potomac_River "Potomac River")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Healy_Pink.jpg/250px-Healy_Pink.jpg)](/wiki/File:Healy_Pink.jpg)

[Healy Hall](/wiki/Healy_Hall "Healy Hall") at sunset

Georgetown's undergraduate and medical school campuses are situated on an elevated site above the [Potomac River](/wiki/Potomac_River "Potomac River") overlooking [Northern Virginia](/wiki/Northern_Virginia "Northern Virginia"). Because of this, Georgetown University is often referred to as "The Hilltop". The main gates, known as the *Healy Gates*, are located at the intersection of 37th and O Streets NW, and lead directly to the heart of campus. The main campus is relatively compact, being 104 acres (0.4 km2) in area, but includes fifty-four buildings, student residences and apartments capable of accommodating 80% of undergraduates, and various athletic facilities.[[134]](#cite_note-141) Campus green areas include fountains, [a cemetery](/wiki/Jesuit_Community_Cemetery_(Georgetown_University) "Jesuit Community Cemetery (Georgetown University)"), large clusters of flowers, groves of trees, and open quadrangles.[[135]](#cite_note-142) New buildings and major renovations are required to meet [LEED](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environmental_Design "Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design") Silver criteria,[[136]](#cite_note-143) and the campus was nominated for the District Sustainability People's Choice Award in 2018.[[137]](#cite_note-144)

Healy Hall, designed by [Paul J. Pelz](/wiki/Paul_J._Pelz "Paul J. Pelz") in Neo-Medieval style and built from 1877 to 1879, is the architectural gem of Georgetown's campus, and is a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark").[[138]](#cite_note-145) Within Healy Hall are a number of notable rooms including [Gaston Hall](/wiki/Gaston_Hall "Gaston Hall"), Riggs Library, and the Bioethics Library Hirst Reading Room.[[139]](#cite_note-146) Both Healy Hall and the [Georgetown University Astronomical Observatory](/wiki/Georgetown_University_Astronomical_Observatory "Georgetown University Astronomical Observatory"), built in 1844, are listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places").[[140]](#cite_note-nhl-147)

In addition to the front lawn, the main campus has traditionally centered on Dahlgren Quadrangle behind Healy Hall, which is home to Dahlgren Chapel; however, in recent decades, Red Square has replaced the Dahlgren Quadrangle as the focus of student life.[[141]](#cite_note-148) North of Red Square is an extended pathway that is home to buildings such as the Intercultural Center (ICC), the Reiss Science building, the newly constructed dormitory named after [Pedro Arrupe](/wiki/Pedro_Arrupe "Pedro Arrupe"), and the large Leavey Student Center.[[142]](#cite_note-149)

The medical school is located in the northwestern part of the main campus on Reservoir Road. It is integrated with Georgetown University Hospital.[[143]](#cite_note-map-150)

In the 21st-century, the West side of the Hilltop has emerged as a newly developing area of the main campus. The university completed the Southwest Quadrangle Project in late 2003 and brought a new 907-bed upperclassmen residence hall, the Leo J. O'Donovan dining hall, a large underground parking facility, and a new Jesuit Residence to the campus.[[144]](#cite_note-151) The school's first [performing arts center](/wiki/Performing_arts_center "Performing arts center"), named for [Royden B. Davis](/wiki/Royden_B._Davis "Royden B. Davis"), was completed in November 2005. The new business school headquarters, named for [Rafik Hariri](/wiki/Rafik_Hariri "Rafik Hariri"), opened in Fall 2009, and Regents Hall, the new science building, opened in Fall 2012. Along with the adjacent Leavey Student Center, these two large buildings have become popular study spaces and overlook a newly developed scenic lawn space.[[145]](#cite_note-152)

In the fall of 2014, the university opened a new student center, the Healey Family Student Center (HFSC) to complement the longstanding Leavey Center. The Healey Family Student Center is located on the first floor of [New South Hall](/wiki/New_South_Hall "New South Hall"), a space that functioned as the university's main dining facility until the Leo J. O'Donovan dining hall opening in 2003. It features over 43,000 square feet including several study spaces, conference rooms, dance, and music studios, as well as a pub called Bulldog Tavern and a salad store Hilltoss, which is operated by [The Corp](/wiki/Students_of_Georgetown,_Inc. "Students of Georgetown, Inc.").[[146]](#cite_note-153)

[![Wide-angle view of the campus running along the Potomac River](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Georgetown_University_Panorama_04_2012_1394.jpg/960px-Georgetown_University_Panorama_04_2012_1394.jpg)](/wiki/File:Georgetown_University_Panorama_04_2012_1394.jpg "A panoramic photo of the campus along the Potomac River seen from Key Bridge, which connects Georgetown with Rosslyn, Virginia")

A panoramic photo of the campus along the [Potomac River](/wiki/Potomac_River "Potomac River") seen from [Key Bridge](/wiki/Key_Bridge_(Washington,_D.C.) "Key Bridge (Washington, D.C.)"), which connects [Georgetown](/wiki/Georgetown_(Washington,_D.C.) "Georgetown (Washington, D.C.)") with [Rosslyn, Virginia](/wiki/Rosslyn,_Virginia "Rosslyn, Virginia")

As a location, Georgetown was ranked nationally as the second-best [college town](/wiki/College_town "College town") by [The Princeton Review](/wiki/The_Princeton_Review "The Princeton Review") in 2011.[[147]](#cite_note-princeton-154) The Georgetown neighborhood west of Wisconsin Avenue NW is dominated by the presence of university students. Students have easy access to the M Street commercial area, the Georgetown Waterfront, and numerous trails that lead to the National Mall and other parks. Despite this, "[town and gown](/wiki/Town_and_gown "Town and gown")" relations between the university communities and other Georgetown residents are often strained by facilities construction, enlargement of the student body, as well as noise and alcohol violations.[[148]](#cite_note-155) Several groups of neighborhood residents have attempted to slow University growth in Georgetown, creating friction between students and the surrounding neighborhood. Despite the relative safety of the neighborhood, crime is a persistent issue, with campus security responding to 257 crimes in 2008, the majority of which were petty crimes.[[149]](#cite_note-156)

### Law Center campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Law Center campus")]

Main article: [Georgetown University Law Center](/wiki/Georgetown_University_Law_Center "Georgetown University Law Center")

[![Two modern glass and concrete building side by side in front of an open grass lawn which has a short clocktower on the left side.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Georgetown_Law_Campus.JPG/250px-Georgetown_Law_Campus.JPG)](/wiki/File:Georgetown_Law_Campus.JPG)

[Georgetown Law School](/wiki/Georgetown_University_Law_Center "Georgetown University Law Center")'s campus on [Capitol Hill](/wiki/Capitol_Hill "Capitol Hill")

The [Law Center campus](/wiki/Georgetown_University_Law_Center#Campus "Georgetown University Law Center") is located in the [Capitol Hill neighborhood](/wiki/Capitol_Hill,_Washington,_D.C. "Capitol Hill, Washington, D.C.") on New Jersey Avenue, near [Washington Union Station](/wiki/Washington_Union_Station "Washington Union Station"), and consists of five buildings. First-year students at the Law Center can live in the single on-campus dormitory, the Gewirz Student Center.[[150]](#cite_note-157) Most second- and third-year students, as well as some first-year students, live off-campus. As there is little housing near the Law Center, most are spread throughout the Washington metropolitan area.[[151]](#cite_note-158)

The Campus Completion Project, finished in 2005, saw the addition of the Hotung International Building and the Sport and Fitness Center. G Street and F Street are closed off between 1st and 2nd Streets to create open lawns flanking McDonough Hall, the main building on the campus.[[152]](#cite_note-ccp-159) In 2019, the university purchased $70 million of a building at 500 First St. NW to add to the Georgetown University Law Center. Opening in 2020, the 130,000-square-foot edifice will provide classrooms and offices for researchers in health, technology, law and other fields.[[153]](#cite_note-160)

### Downtown campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Downtown campus")]

Main article: [Georgetown University School of Continuing Studies](/wiki/Georgetown_University_School_of_Continuing_Studies "Georgetown University School of Continuing Studies")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/d/da/SCSJPG.jpeg/250px-SCSJPG.jpeg)](/wiki/File:SCSJPG.jpeg)

In 2013, [Georgetown University School of Continuing Studies](/wiki/Georgetown_University_School_of_Continuing_Studies "Georgetown University School of Continuing Studies") opened in [Downtown Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Downtown,_Washington,_D.C. "Downtown, Washington, D.C.")

The [School of Continuing Studies](/wiki/Georgetown_University_School_of_Continuing_Studies "Georgetown University School of Continuing Studies") (SCS) campus is located in a 95,000 square foot, state-of-the-art building in downtown Washington, D.C. The campus currently serves as the home for Georgetown's graduate programs in fields such as Applied Intelligence, Journalism, Public Relations, Real Estate, Sports Industry Management, and Urban & Regional Planning.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The current building, which was completed in 2013, includes 30 classrooms, a 125-person auditorium, a digital media lab, a broadcast studio, an interfaith chapel, and a dedicated library. It is located in the [Chinatown neighborhood](/wiki/Chinatown_(Washington,_D.C.) "Chinatown (Washington, D.C.)") of the city and is considered to be one of the most accessible locations in town, with a Transit Score of 100 and a Walk Score of 98. It is also located just a few blocks away from the [Capital One Arena](/wiki/Capital_One_Arena "Capital One Arena"), the home court of the men's basketball team.[[154]](#cite_note-161)

### Qatar campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Qatar campus")]

Main article: [Georgetown University in Qatar](/wiki/Georgetown_University_in_Qatar "Georgetown University in Qatar")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Entrance_to_Georgetown_University_in_Education_City.jpg/250px-Entrance_to_Georgetown_University_in_Education_City.jpg)](/wiki/File:Entrance_to_Georgetown_University_in_Education_City.jpg)

Entrance to Georgetown University's Qatar Campus in Doha Education City

In 2002, the [Qatar Foundation for Education, Science and Community Development](/wiki/Qatar_Foundation_for_Education,_Science_and_Community_Development "Qatar Foundation for Education, Science and Community Development") presented the School of Foreign Service with the resources and space to open a campus in [Education City](/wiki/Education_City "Education City") in [Al Rayyan](/wiki/Al_Rayyan_(city) "Al Rayyan (city)"), Qatar.[[155]](#cite_note-162) SFS-Qatar opened in 2005 as a liberal arts and international affairs undergraduate school for regional students.[[156]](#cite_note-163) It offers the same [Bachelor of Science in Foreign Service](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Science_in_Foreign_Service "Bachelor of Science in Foreign Service") (BSFS) as the main campus, in addition to three certificates. Apart from language courses, including Arabic and French, all courses are taught in English and the curriculum and course materials in the specified majors are identical to those offered at Georgetown's main campus in Washington D.C.[[157]](#cite_note-164)

### Facilities abroad

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Facilities abroad")]

[![A yellow stucco building with a red clay roof and several arched doors and windows, surrounded by green shrubs.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Le_balze%2C_giardino_d%27inverno_02.JPG/250px-Le_balze%2C_giardino_d%27inverno_02.JPG)](/wiki/File:Le_balze,_giardino_d%27inverno_02.JPG)

[Villa Le Balze](/wiki/Villa_Le_Balze "Villa Le Balze") in [Fiesole](/wiki/Fiesole "Fiesole"), Italy, hosting interdisciplinary studies

In December 1979, the [Marquesa Margaret Rockefeller de Larrain](/wiki/Margaret_Rockefeller_Strong_de_Larra%C3%ADn,_Marquesa_de_Cuevas "Margaret Rockefeller Strong de Larraín, Marquesa de Cuevas"), granddaughter of [John D. Rockefeller](/wiki/John_D._Rockefeller "John D. Rockefeller"), gave the Villa Le Balze to Georgetown University.[[158]](#cite_note-165) The Villa is in Fiesole, Italy, on a hill above the city of [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"). The Villa is used year-round for study abroad programs focused on specialized [interdisciplinary](/wiki/Interdisciplinary "Interdisciplinary") study of [Italian culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Italy "Culture of Italy") and civilization.[[159]](#cite_note-166)

The main facility for the [McGhee Center for Eastern Mediterranean Studies](/wiki/McGhee_Center_for_Eastern_Mediterranean_Studies "McGhee Center for Eastern Mediterranean Studies") was donated to Georgetown in 1989 by alumnus and former [United States Ambassador to Turkey](/wiki/United_States_Ambassador_to_Turkey "United States Ambassador to Turkey") [George C. McGhee](/wiki/George_C._McGhee "George C. McGhee").[[160]](#cite_note-167) The school is in the town of Alanya, Turkey within the [Seljuq](/wiki/Seljuq_dynasty "Seljuq dynasty")-era [Alanya Castle](/wiki/Alanya_Castle "Alanya Castle"), on the Mediterranean. The center operates study abroad programs one semester each year, concentrating on [Turkish language](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"), [architectural history](/wiki/Architectural_history "Architectural history"), and [Islamic studies](/wiki/Islamic_studies "Islamic studies").[[161]](#cite_note-168)

In December 2007, Georgetown opened a liaison office in Shanghai, China to coordinate with [Fudan University](/wiki/Fudan_University "Fudan University") and others.[[162]](#cite_note-169) In 2008, the Georgetown University Law Center in conjunction with an international consortium of law schools established the [Center for Transnational Legal Studies](/wiki/Center_for_Transnational_Legal_Studies "Center for Transnational Legal Studies") in London, England.[[163]](#cite_note-170)

In November 2023, [Indonesian President](/wiki/President_of_Indonesia "President of Indonesia") [Joko Widodo](/wiki/Joko_Widodo "Joko Widodo") announced Georgetown plans to open a [satellite campus](/wiki/Satellite_campus "Satellite campus") of the School of Foreign Service in [Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta") that will offer degree programs for present and future policymakers in the United States and [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia").[[164]](#cite_note-171)[[165]](#cite_note-172)[[166]](#cite_note-173)[[167]](#cite_note-174) The campus, known as [Georgetown SFS Asia-Pacific](/wiki/Georgetown_SFS_Asia-Pacific "Georgetown SFS Asia-Pacific") (GSAP), was launched in January 2025.[[168]](#cite_note-:3-175)[[169]](#cite_note-176)

See also: [Housing at Georgetown University](/wiki/Housing_at_Georgetown_University "Housing at Georgetown University")
