[![Many students mingle in the background while a group sit in the foreground on a grass lawn. The large stone clocktower is seen above the trees on the lawn.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Georgetown_Day.jpg/250px-Georgetown_Day.jpg)](/wiki/File:Georgetown_Day.jpg)

Students celebrate Georgetown Day in late spring with a campus carnival

The Georgetown undergraduate student body, at 6,926 as of 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit), is composed primarily of students from outside the District of Columbia area, with 33% of new 2016 students coming from the [Mid-Atlantic states](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_states "Mid-Atlantic states"), 11% being international students, and the remainder coming from other areas of the U.S.[[171]](#cite_note-admissions-180) The student body also represented 129 countries, with 11% being [international](/wiki/International_student "International student"),[[172]](#cite_note-181) including over 330 undergraduate and 1,050 graduate students who chose to come to Georgetown as a study abroad destination in 2009–10.[[173]](#cite_note-internationals-182) In 2014–2015, the racial diversity of the undergraduate student body was 57.0% [white](/wiki/White_people "White people"), 8.8% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_people "Asian people"), 6.2% [black](/wiki/Black_people "Black people"), and 7.5% [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic "Hispanic"). The median family income of Georgetown students is $229,100, with 51% of students coming from the top 5% highest-earning families and 13.5% from the bottom 60%.[[174]](#cite_note-NYT_mobility_index-183) 55.1% of undergraduates are female.[[175]](#cite_note-NCES_2014-2015-184)

Although it is a Jesuit university, only 41% of the student body identify as [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), while 22% identify as [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") as of 2009[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit).[[176]](#cite_note-SCU-185) Georgetown employs a full-time [rabbi](/wiki/Rabbi "Rabbi"), as 6.5% of undergraduates are [Jewish](/wiki/Jew "Jew").[[176]](#cite_note-SCU-185) It was the first U.S. college to have a full-time [imam](/wiki/Imam "Imam"), to serve the over four hundred [Muslims](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") on campus,[[177]](#cite_note-186) and in 2014, they appointed their first Hindu priest to serve a weekly community of around one hundred.[[178]](#cite_note-187) Georgetown also sponsors student groups for [Baháʼí](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith"), [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), and [Mormon](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") traditions.[[179]](#cite_note-188) The student body consists of both religious and non-religious students, and more than four-hundred [freshmen](/wiki/First_year "First year") and transfer students attend a [nonreligious](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion") [Ignatian](/wiki/Spiritual_Exercises_of_Ignatius_of_Loyola "Spiritual Exercises of Ignatius of Loyola") [retreat](/wiki/Retreat_(spiritual) "Retreat (spiritual)"), called ESCAPE, annually.[[180]](#cite_note-189)[[181]](#cite_note-190)

A 2007 survey of undergraduates also suggests that 62.8% are sexually active, and 6.2% identify as [LGBTQ](/wiki/LGBTQ "LGBTQ").[[176]](#cite_note-SCU-185)[[182]](#cite_note-191) Three quarters of a 2009 survey considered [homophobia](/wiki/Homophobia "Homophobia") a campus problem.[[183]](#cite_note-192) *[Newsweek](/wiki/Newsweek "Newsweek")*, however, rated Georgetown among its top "Gay-Friendly Schools" in 2010.[[184]](#cite_note-193)

A survey by the school in 2016 showed that 31% of female undergraduates reported experiencing unwanted sexual contact, and 86% of LGBTQ students reported some form of sexual harassment at the college.[[185]](#cite_note-194) In 2011, *College Magazine* ranked Georgetown as the tenth most [hipster](/wiki/Hipster_(contemporary_subculture) "Hipster (contemporary subculture)") U.S. college,[[186]](#cite_note-195) while [People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals](/wiki/People_for_the_Ethical_Treatment_of_Animals "People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals") considered it the third most [vegan](/wiki/Vegan "Vegan") friendly small U.S. school.[[187]](#cite_note-196)

Almost all undergraduates attend full-time.[[188]](#cite_note-nces2-197) A majority of undergraduates, 76%, live on-campus in several dormitories and apartment complexes, including all underclassmen.[[189]](#cite_note-198) As of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit), 1255 undergraduates and 339 graduate students live off-campus, mostly in the [Georgetown](/wiki/Georgetown,_Washington,_D.C. "Georgetown, Washington, D.C."), [Glover Park](/wiki/Glover_Park,_Washington,_D.C. "Glover Park, Washington, D.C."), [Burleith](/wiki/Burleith "Burleith"), and [Foxhall](/wiki/Foxhall,_Washington,_D.C. "Foxhall, Washington, D.C.") neighborhoods.[[190]](#cite_note-199) Since Fall 2022, housing is available for on-campus graduate students at [55 H St. NW](/wiki/H_Street_(Washington,_D.C.) "H Street (Washington, D.C.)"), which is 30 minute from the Hilltop campus via the Georgetown University Transportation Shuttle (GUTS).[[191]](#cite_note-Housing_at_55_H_St-200) Students at the Law Center are accommodated at the Gewirz Student Center. All students in the Medical School live off-campus, most in the surrounding neighborhoods, with some in Northern Virginia and elsewhere through the DMV region.[[192]](#cite_note-somsl-201)

### Student groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Student groups")]

[![Two young white men sit at a table with several teenage African American students mingle around it, and one signs a paper on it. Also on the table is a laptop.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Money_Matters_at_TMA.jpg/250px-Money_Matters_at_TMA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Money_Matters_at_TMA.jpg)

Students volunteering at a Washington, D.C. school

As of 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit), 92.9% of Georgetown University undergraduates are involved in at least one of the 179 registered student organizations which cover a variety of interests: student government, club sports, media and publications, performing arts, religion, and volunteer and service.[[193]](#cite_note-202) Students also operate campus stores, banks, and medical services. Students often find their interests at the Student Activities Commission Club Fair, where both official and unofficial organizations set up tables.[[194]](#cite_note-203) The [Georgetown University Student Association](/wiki/Georgetown_University_Student_Association "Georgetown University Student Association") is the student government organization for undergraduates. There are also elected student representatives within the schools that serve on Academic councils, as well as to the university Board of Directors, and, since 1996, to the Georgetown [Advisory Neighborhood Commission](/wiki/Advisory_Neighborhood_Commission "Advisory Neighborhood Commission").[[195]](#cite_note-204)

Georgetown's student organizations include one of the nation's oldest debating clubs, the [Philodemic Society](/wiki/Philodemic_Society "Philodemic Society"), founded in 1830,[[196]](#cite_note-205) and the oldest university theater group, the [Mask and Bauble Dramatic Society](/wiki/Mask_and_Bauble_Dramatic_Society "Mask and Bauble Dramatic Society").[[197]](#cite_note-206) [Nomadic Theatre](/wiki/Nomadic_Theatre "Nomadic Theatre") was founded in 1982 as an alternative troupe without an on-campus home.[[198]](#cite_note-207) The Georgetown Improv Association, founded in 1995, performs [improvisational](/wiki/Improvisational_theatre "Improvisational theatre") shows on-campus at Bulldog Alley in addition to hosting "Improvfest", one of the [oldest improv festivals in the country](/wiki/List_of_improvisational_theater_festivals "List of improvisational theater festivals").[[199]](#cite_note-208)

The [Model United Nations](/wiki/Model_United_Nations "Model United Nations") team that is run by the Georgetown International Relations Club, the largest club on campus, and its affiliate, Georgetown International Relations Association, has attained the status of best in the world on several occasions.[[200]](#cite_note-209)

There are seven *[a cappella](/wiki/A_cappella "A cappella")* groups on campus, including The Georgetown Saxatones, [The Georgetown Chimes](/wiki/Georgetown_Chimes "Georgetown Chimes"), the Phantoms, Superfood, The GraceNotes, the Chamber Singers, Essence, Harmony, and the Capitol G's.[[201]](#cite_note-210) These groups perform annually at the "D.C. A Cappella Festival", held since 1991; the "Cherry Tree Massacre" concert series, held since 1974; and "Spring Sing", held since 2011.[[202]](#cite_note-211)[[203]](#cite_note-212)[[204]](#cite_note-213) The Georgetown University Band is composed of the Georgetown Pep Band and the Georgetown Wind Ensemble, and performs on campus, in Washington, D.C., and at post-season basketball tournaments.[[205]](#cite_note-214)

In addition to student organizations and clubs, Georgetown University is home to one of the nation's largest entirely student-owned and -operated corporations, [Students of Georgetown, Inc.](/wiki/Students_of_Georgetown,_Inc. "Students of Georgetown, Inc.")[[206]](#cite_note-corp-215) Founded in 1972, "The Corp" operates three [coffee shops](/wiki/Coffeehouse "Coffeehouse"), two [grocery stores](/wiki/Grocery_stores "Grocery stores"), the Hilltoss, a concept similar to [Sweetgreen](/wiki/Sweetgreen "Sweetgreen"), catering services, and seasonal storage for students.[[206]](#cite_note-corp-215) The business has annual revenues of about $5 million,[[207]](#cite_note-216) and surpluses are directly re-invested into the Georgetown student body through Corp Philanthropy, which gave out over $85,000 in scholarships and donations to Georgetown groups in 2014–2015.[[208]](#cite_note-217) [Georgetown University Alumni & Student Federal Credit Union](/wiki/Georgetown_University_Alumni_%26_Student_Federal_Credit_Union "Georgetown University Alumni & Student Federal Credit Union") is the largest all student-run credit union in the United States, with over $17 million in assets and 12,000 members.[[209]](#cite_note-218) The Georgetown University Student Investment Fund is one of a few undergraduate-run [investment funds](/wiki/Investment_fund "Investment fund") in the United States, and hosted CNBC's [Jim Cramer](/wiki/Jim_Cramer "Jim Cramer") to tape *[Mad Money](/wiki/Mad_Money "Mad Money")* in September 2006.[[210]](#cite_note-219) Hilltop Consultants is a student-run nonprofit [consulting](/wiki/Consulting "Consulting") agency that works with local and international organizations including [Teach For America](/wiki/Teach_For_America "Teach For America"), [Habitat for Humanity](/wiki/Habitat_for_Humanity "Habitat for Humanity"), and [Special Olympics](/wiki/Special_Olympics "Special Olympics").[[211]](#cite_note-220) The Hilltop Microfinance Initiative is a student-run [micro-finance](/wiki/Microfinance "Microfinance") organization, aiming to empower [underserved](/wiki/Underserved "Underserved") communities in DC, Maryland, and Virginia through [small business loans](/wiki/Small_business_loans "Small business loans") and financial coaching.[[212]](#cite_note-221)

Another student-run group, the Georgetown Emergency Response Medical Service, "GERMS", is an all-volunteer ambulance service founded in 1982 that serves campus and the surrounding communities.[[213]](#cite_note-222) Georgetown's [Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") [Reserve Officer Training Corps](/wiki/Reserve_Officer_Training_Corps "Reserve Officer Training Corps") (ROTC) unit, the Hoya Battalion, is the oldest military unit native to the District of Columbia,[[214]](#cite_note-223) and was awarded the top ranking among ROTC programs in 2012.[[215]](#cite_note-224) The proportion of ROTC students at Georgetown was the 79th highest among universities in the United States as of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit).[[216]](#cite_note-225) GUGS, the Georgetown University Grilling Society, has been a Georgetown tradition since 2002, selling half-pound hamburgers in Red Square on most Fridays.[[217]](#cite_note-226)

### Activism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Activism")]

[![The intercultural center looms behind Red Square. Dozens of students are pictured in the plaza, many passing through, others sitting at tables demonstrating](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Intercultural_Center.jpg/250px-Intercultural_Center.jpg)](/wiki/File:Intercultural_Center.jpg)

Students demonstrate and pass through Red Square, the center of student activism on Georgetown University's campus

Georgetown University student organizations include a diverse array of groups focused on social justice issues, including organizations run through both Student Affairs and the [Center for Social Justice Research, Teaching, & Service](/wiki/Center_for_Social_Justice_Research,_Teaching,_%26_Service "Center for Social Justice Research, Teaching, & Service") (CSJ). The latter organization, founded in 2001, works to integrate into their education Georgetown's founding mission of education in service for justice and the common good.[[218]](#cite_note-227)

Oriented against gender violence, [Take Back the Night](/wiki/Take_Back_the_Night_(protest) "Take Back the Night (protest)") coordinates an annual rally and march to protest against rape and other forms of violence against women.[[219]](#cite_note-228) Georgetown Solidarity Committee is a [workers' rights](/wiki/Labor_rights "Labor rights") organization whose successes include ending use of sweatshops in producing Georgetown-logoed apparel, and garnering pay raises for both university cleaning staff and police.[[220]](#cite_note-229) Georgetown Students for [Fair Trade](/wiki/Fair_trade "Fair trade") successfully advocated for all coffee in campus cafeterias to be [Fair Trade Certified](/wiki/Fairtrade_certification "Fairtrade certification").[[221]](#cite_note-230)

Georgetown has many additional groups representing national, ethnic, and linguistic interests. Georgetown has the second-most politically active student body in the United States according to [The Princeton Review](/wiki/The_Princeton_Review "The Princeton Review").[[147]](#cite_note-princeton-154) Groups based on local, national, and international issues are popular, and political speech is protected on campus. Student political organizations are active on campus and engage their many members in local and national politics. The Georgetown University College Republicans represent their party, while the Georgetown University College Democrats, the largest student organization on campus in 2008, represent theirs.[[222]](#cite_note-231)

As a Catholic university, the [pro-life](/wiki/Anti-abortion_movements "Anti-abortion movements") organization Georgetown University Right to Life is officially recognized by the university.[[223]](#cite_note-232) In 1981, Right to Life students helped found The Northwest Center, one of two crisis pregnancy centers in Washington. Every year, the organization sends a delegation to the [March for Life](/wiki/March_for_Life_(Washington,_D.C.) "March for Life (Washington, D.C.)") to show support for the national [pro-life](/wiki/Anti-abortion_movements "Anti-abortion movements") movement.[[224]](#cite_note-233) In addition, every January since 2000 the club has organized the [Cardinal O'Connor Conference on Life](/wiki/Cardinal_O%27Connor_Conference_on_Life "Cardinal O'Connor Conference on Life"). It is the largest student-organized [pro-life](/wiki/Anti-abortion_movements "Anti-abortion movements") conference in the United States.[[225]](#cite_note-234)

The pro-abortion organization H\*yas for Choice is not officially recognized by the university as its positions on [abortion](/wiki/Abortion "Abortion") are in opposition to university policy, including supporting late-term abortion as is still legal in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Abortion_in_the_District_of_Columbia "Abortion in the District of Columbia"), prompting the asterisk in "H\*yas".[[226]](#cite_note-235) While not financially supported by the school, the organization is permitted to meet and table in university spaces.[[227]](#cite_note-236)

Georgetown is also home to a number of student organizations focused on [sustainability](/wiki/Sustainability "Sustainability") and [environmentalism](/wiki/Environmentalism "Environmentalism").[[228]](#cite_note-237) GREEN, the Georgetown Renewable Energy and Environmental Network, is the largest of these groups. Another student group, GU Fossil Free, was founded in 2013, and aimed to pressure the university to divest its endowment from fossil fuels.[[229]](#cite_note-238) Georgetown is a member of the Ivy Plus Sustainability Consortium, through which it has committed to best-practice sharing and the ongoing exchange of campus sustainability solutions along with the other member institutions; it hosted the annual Ivy Plus summit in 2019.[[230]](#cite_note-239)[[231]](#cite_note-240) The university announced in February 2020 that it would fully divest its endowment from fossil fuels within the next 5 years.[[232]](#cite_note-241)

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Media")]

[![Several young men and women sit in office chairs working at computers around a room where the walls are covered in printed pages. A central wood table and bookcases are featured.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/The_Hoya_office.jpg/250px-The_Hoya_office.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Hoya_office.jpg)

The Leavey Center office of *[The Hoya](/wiki/The_Hoya "The Hoya")*, the university student newspaper founded in 1920

Georgetown University has several student-run newspapers and academic journals. *[The Hoya](/wiki/The_Hoya "The Hoya")* is the university's oldest newspaper. It has been in print since 1920, and since 1987, has been published twice weekly.[[233]](#cite_note-242) *[The Georgetown Voice](/wiki/The_Georgetown_Voice "The Georgetown Voice")*, known for its weekly cover stories, is a [newsmagazine](/wiki/Newsmagazine "Newsmagazine") that was founded in March 1969 to focus more attention on citywide and national issues.[[234]](#cite_note-243) *The Georgetown Independent* is a monthly journal of news, commentary and the arts.[[235]](#cite_note-244) Founded in 1966, the *[Georgetown Law Weekly](/wiki/Georgetown_Law_Weekly "Georgetown Law Weekly")* is the student-run paper on the Law Center campus, and is a three-time winner of the [American Bar Association](/wiki/American_Bar_Association "American Bar Association")'s Best Newspaper award.[[236]](#cite_note-245) Established in 1995, the *[Georgetown Public Policy Review](/wiki/Georgetown_Public_Policy_Review "Georgetown Public Policy Review")* is a student-run journal based out of the McCourt School of Public Policy that publishes online articles and a peer-reviewed spring edition.[[237]](#cite_note-246) The *[Georgetown Journal of International Affairs](/wiki/Georgetown_Journal_of_International_Affairs "Georgetown Journal of International Affairs")*, established in 2000, is a student-managed, peer-reviewed journal that publishes perspectives on current affairs and international relations from experts such as heads of states and renowned professors; it is the official journal of the School of Foreign Service and published by [Johns Hopkins University Press](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University_Press "Johns Hopkins University Press").[[238]](#cite_note-247)

*The Georgetown Academy*, restarted in 2008 after a hiatus, targets traditionalist [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") readers, while the *Georgetown Review*, founded in 2016, aims to bring a [conservative](/wiki/Conservatism_in_the_United_States "Conservatism in the United States") and [libertarian](/wiki/Libertarianism "Libertarianism") viewpoint to campus.[[239]](#cite_note-academy-248)[[240]](#cite_note-249) Other political publications include the *Georgetown Progressive*, an online publication run by the Georgetown University College Democrats, and *Counterpoint Magazine*, a liberal monthly founded in the spring of 2011.[[241]](#cite_note-250) *The Fire This Time* is Georgetown's minority news source.[[242]](#cite_note-251) *[The Georgetown Heckler](/wiki/The_Georgetown_Heckler "The Georgetown Heckler")* is a humor magazine founded on the Internet in 2003 by Georgetown students, releasing its first print issue in 2007.[[243]](#cite_note-252)

The university has a campus-wide television station, GUTV, which began broadcasting in 1999. The station hosts an annual student [film festival](/wiki/Film_festival "Film festival") in April for campus filmmakers.[[244]](#cite_note-253) [WGTB](/wiki/WGTB "WGTB"), Georgetown's radio station, is available as a [webcast](/wiki/Webcast "Webcast") and on 92.3 FM in certain dormitories. The station was founded in 1946, and broadcast on 90.1 FM from 1960 to 1979, when university president [Timothy S. Healy](/wiki/Timothy_S._Healy "Timothy S. Healy") gave away the frequency and broadcast capabilities to the [University of the District of Columbia](/wiki/University_of_the_District_of_Columbia "University of the District of Columbia") because of WGTB's [far left](/wiki/Far_left "Far left") political orientation. The station now broadcasts through the Internet in its headquarters in the Leavey Center.[[245]](#cite_note-254)

### Greek life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Greek life")]

Many Jesuit schools choose to disassociate from Greek systems, and Georgetown University officially recognizes and funds only one of the many Greek organizations on campus, [Alpha Phi Omega](/wiki/Alpha_Phi_Omega "Alpha Phi Omega"), the national co-ed community service fraternity. Other Greek organizations exist on campus, although none require members to live in Greek housing.[[246]](#cite_note-255) Additionally, Georgetown University students are affiliated, in some cases, with fraternities at other nearby universities and colleges.[[247]](#cite_note-256)

About 10 percent of undergraduate students participate in Greek life, a ratio lower than at many other colleges and universities.[[248]](#cite_note-257)[[249]](#cite_note-258)

### Traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Traditions")]

[![Four men in suits sit on chairs on a red stage in front of ornate gold and brown wall.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Obama_at_Georgetown_University_talk_on_poverty.jpg/250px-Obama_at_Georgetown_University_talk_on_poverty.jpg)](/wiki/File:Obama_at_Georgetown_University_talk_on_poverty.jpg)

Gaston Hall, a venue for events, including this May 2015 speech by former U.S. President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama")

Annual events on campus celebrate Georgetown traditions, culture, alumni, sports, and politics. In late April, Georgetown University celebrates Georgetown Day.[[250]](#cite_note-259)

[Homecoming](/wiki/Homecoming "Homecoming") coincides with a home football game, and festivities such as tailgating and a formal dance are sponsored by the Alumni Association to draw past graduates back to campus.[[251]](#cite_note-260) The largest planned sports related celebration is the first basketball practice of the season. Dubbed *Midnight Madness*, this event introduces the men's and women's basketball teams shortly after midnight on the first day the teams are allowed by NCAA rules to formally practice together. The festivities include a dunk contest, a 3-point contest, a scrimmage, and a musical act.[[252]](#cite_note-261)

## Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Athletics")]

Main article: [Georgetown Hoyas](/wiki/Georgetown_Hoyas "Georgetown Hoyas")

[![A African-American teenage basketball player wearing a gray uniform looks over his shoulder at another playing in a blue uniform. Behind them are fans and a basketball hoop with the word "HOYAS" on it.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Roy_Hibbert_in_2006.jpg/250px-Roy_Hibbert_in_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Roy_Hibbert_in_2006.jpg)

[Georgetown basketball](/wiki/Georgetown_Hoyas_men%27s_basketball "Georgetown Hoyas men's basketball") players, including [Roy Hibbert](/wiki/Roy_Hibbert "Roy Hibbert"), pictured in December 2006, have led the Hoyas to eight [Big East Conference](/wiki/Big_East_Conference "Big East Conference") championships

Georgetown fields 23 varsity athletic teams and an additional 23 athletic club teams. The university's varsity teams participate in the [NCAA](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association")'s [Division I](/wiki/Division_I_(NCAA) "Division I (NCAA)"). The school competes in the [Big East Conference](/wiki/Big_East_Conference "Big East Conference") in most sports. Exceptions include the [football team](/wiki/Georgetown_Hoyas_football "Georgetown Hoyas football"), which competes in [Division I FCS](/wiki/Division_I_FCS "Division I FCS")' [Patriot League](/wiki/Patriot_League "Patriot League"), the sailing team, which competes in the [Middle Atlantic Intercollegiate Sailing Association](/wiki/Middle_Atlantic_Intercollegiate_Sailing_Association "Middle Atlantic Intercollegiate Sailing Association"), and the rowing teams, which competes in the [Eastern Association of Rowing Colleges](/wiki/Eastern_Association_of_Rowing_Colleges "Eastern Association of Rowing Colleges").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In March 2002, *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")* listed Georgetown's athletics program among the 20 best in the nation.[[253]](#cite_note-262) Georgetown's student athletes have a 94% graduation success rate,[[254]](#cite_note-263) and over 100 have gone on to play at some level of professional athletics.[[255]](#cite_note-264)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Hoya_logo.gif/250px-Hoya_logo.gif)](/wiki/File:Hoya_logo.gif)

Hoya, the Georgetown University's nickname

The school's teams are called "Hoyas", which originated sometime prior to 1893, when students invented the mixed [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") and [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") chant of "[Hoya Saxa](/wiki/Hoya_Saxa "Hoya Saxa")", translating roughly as "what (or such) rocks". The [school's baseball team](/wiki/Georgetown_Hoyas_baseball "Georgetown Hoyas baseball"), then called the Stonewalls, began in 1870, and football began in 1874; the chant likely refers to one of these teams.[[256]](#cite_note-265) By the 1920s, the term "Hoyas" was used to describe groups on campus, and by 1928, campus sports writers started using it instead of the older team name, the "Hilltoppers".[[257]](#cite_note-hoia-266)[[258]](#cite_note-267)

The [men's basketball](/wiki/Georgetown_Hoyas_men%27s_basketball "Georgetown Hoyas men's basketball") team, which won the [NCAA championship](/wiki/NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Basketball_Championship "NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Championship") in [1984](/wiki/1984_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "1984 NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament") under coach [John Thompson](/wiki/John_Thompson_(basketball) "John Thompson (basketball)"), is among the university's most successful athletic programs. The team holds the record for the most [Big East conference tournament](/wiki/Big_East_men%27s_basketball_tournament "Big East men's basketball tournament") titles with eight, and has made thirty [NCAA tournament appearances](/wiki/NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Tournament_Bids_By_School "NCAA Men's Division I Tournament Bids By School").[[259]](#cite_note-espn-268)[[260]](#cite_note-269) Georgetown's [NBA](/wiki/NBA "NBA") alumni are collectively among the highest earners from a single program.[[261]](#cite_note-270)

The sailing team has won 14 national championships and one world championship in match racing since 2001.[[262]](#cite_note-271)[[263]](#cite_note-272) Over this time, the sailing team has graduated 79 All-Americans and six College Sailors of the year.

Georgetown has been nationally successful in both [cross country](/wiki/Cross_country_running "Cross country running") and [track and field](/wiki/Track_and_field "Track and field").[[264]](#cite_note-273) In 2011, the women's cross country team won Georgetown's second team NCAA championship.[[265]](#cite_note-274)[[266]](#cite_note-275)

[The men's](/wiki/Georgetown_Hoyas_men%27s_lacrosse "Georgetown Hoyas men's lacrosse") and [women's lacrosse teams](/wiki/Georgetown_Hoyas_women%27s_lacrosse "Georgetown Hoyas women's lacrosse") have both been ranked in the top ten nationally,[[267]](#cite_note-276)[[268]](#cite_note-277) as have both soccer teams, with the men winning Georgetown's third team national championship in 2019,[[269]](#cite_note-278) and the women making the national quarterfinals in 2010[[270]](#cite_note-279) and the semifinals in 2016.[[271]](#cite_note-280)

The [rugby club team](/wiki/Georgetown_University_Rugby_Football_Club "Georgetown University Rugby Football Club") made it to the Division II Final Four in 2005 and 2009.[[272]](#cite_note-281) In 2019, Georgetown won the women's team championship at the [United States Intercollegiate Boxing Association](/wiki/United_States_Intercollegiate_Boxing_Association "United States Intercollegiate Boxing Association") national tournament held at [Syracuse University](/wiki/Syracuse_University "Syracuse University").[[273]](#cite_note-282)

Former Georgetown tennis coach Gordon "Gordie" Ernst, one of several people implicated in the [2019 college admissions bribery scandal](/wiki/2019_college_admissions_bribery_scandal "2019 college admissions bribery scandal"), is alleged to have facilitated the admission to Georgetown of as many as 12 students through fraudulent means while accepting bribes of up to $950,000.[[274]](#cite_note-283) Ernst had relocated to the [University of Rhode Island](/wiki/University_of_Rhode_Island "University of Rhode Island"), where he was placed on administrative leave after he was charged and arrested. He later pled guilty to conspiracy to commit federal programs bribery, three counts of federal programs bribery, and to filing false tax returns for failing to report many of the bribery payments.[[275]](#cite_note-284)[[276]](#cite_note-285)[[277]](#cite_note-286)

## People

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: People")]

### Employment and graduation outcomes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Employment and graduation outcomes")]

While about 73% of graduates enter the workforce immediately following graduation, many pursue advanced degrees in law, medicine, business, and other fields.[[278]](#cite_note-destination-287) Georgetown alumni pursuing graduate study have been recipients of 32 [Rhodes Scholarships](/wiki/Rhodes_Scholarship "Rhodes Scholarship"),[[279]](#cite_note-288) 46 [Marshall Scholarships](/wiki/Marshall_Scholarship "Marshall Scholarship"),[[280]](#cite_note-289) 33 [Truman Scholarships](/wiki/Harry_S._Truman_Scholarship "Harry S. Truman Scholarship"),[[281]](#cite_note-290) 15 [Mitchell Scholarships](/wiki/Mitchell_Scholarship "Mitchell Scholarship"),[[282]](#cite_note-291) and 12 [Gates Cambridge Scholarships](/wiki/Gates_Cambridge_Scholarship "Gates Cambridge Scholarship").[[283]](#cite_note-292) Georgetown is among the nation's top producers of [Fulbright Scholars](/wiki/Fulbright_Scholars "Fulbright Scholars"), with 565 over its history, and produced more than any other institution in 2020, 2021, 2023, and 2024.[[284]](#cite_note-293)[[285]](#cite_note-294)[[286]](#cite_note-295)[[287]](#cite_note-296) It is also one of the top-ten yearly producers of [Peace Corps](/wiki/Peace_Corps "Peace Corps") volunteers as of 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit).[[288]](#cite_note-297) Georgetown ranks among the top ten U.S. colleges for median graduate income,[[289]](#cite_note-298)[[290]](#cite_note-299) with graduates of the [McDonough School of Business](/wiki/McDonough_School_of_Business "McDonough School of Business") having the highest average starting salaries, at $100,859;[[278]](#cite_note-destination-287)[[291]](#cite_note-300) Georgetown alumni in general have a median starting salary of $87,100 with a median mid-career salary of $179,300, as of 2023-2024.[[292]](#cite_note-301)

Government and international relations are the two most popular undergraduate majors at Georgetown, and many students go on to careers in politics and diplomacy.[[293]](#cite_note-factual-302) Over 450 alumni currently serve as members and staffers on [Capitol Hill](/wiki/United_States_Capitol "United States Capitol").[[294]](#cite_note-303) Georgetown educated more U.S. diplomats than any other university as of 2015, including at least 92 alumni that have attained the [rank of U.S. Ambassador](/wiki/Ambassadors_of_the_United_States "Ambassadors of the United States").[[295]](#cite_note-gmu-304)[[296]](#cite_note-:2-305) Twelve foreign heads of state and government have graduated from Georgetown.[[296]](#cite_note-:2-305)[[297]](#cite_note-306) Georgetown alumni have served as [foreign ministers](/wiki/Foreign_ministers "Foreign ministers") in a dozen countries.[[298]](#cite_note-307)

Finance and economics are the third and fourth-most popular undergraduate majors,[[293]](#cite_note-factual-302) and 40% of graduates start careers at [consulting](/wiki/Consulting_firm "Consulting firm") or [financial services firms](/wiki/Financial_services "Financial services").[[278]](#cite_note-destination-287) The university is among the top ten alma maters reported by current [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street") banking employees as of June 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit), according to [LinkedIn](/wiki/LinkedIn "LinkedIn") surveys.[[299]](#cite_note-308) Several graduates have gone on take research and leadership positions in many [monetary authorities](/wiki/Central_bank "Central bank") and [international financial institutions](/wiki/International_financial_institutions "International financial institutions"), including [Chair of the Federal Reserve](/wiki/Chair_of_the_Federal_Reserve "Chair of the Federal Reserve") and [President of the World Bank](/wiki/President_of_the_World_Bank_Group "President of the World Bank Group").[[300]](#cite_note-309)[[301]](#cite_note-310)[[302]](#cite_note-311)[[303]](#cite_note-312)

### Notable alumni and faculty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Notable alumni and faculty")]

Main article: [List of Georgetown University alumni](/wiki/List_of_Georgetown_University_alumni "List of Georgetown University alumni")

[Former U.S. President Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") speaking at the 25 year reunion for the class of 1968

[Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton"), 42nd [President of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"), is a 1968 graduate of the [School of Foreign Service](/wiki/Walsh_School_of_Foreign_Service "Walsh School of Foreign Service"). Former officials of the [United States Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_States "Cabinet of the United States") include 59th [Secretary of State](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_State "United States Secretary of State") and former [Supreme Allied Commander Europe](/wiki/Supreme_Allied_Commander_Europe "Supreme Allied Commander Europe"), Gen. [Alexander Haig](/wiki/Alexander_Haig "Alexander Haig"); 22nd [Secretary of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_Defense "United States Secretary of Defense") and former [CIA Director](/wiki/CIA_Director "CIA Director") [Robert Gates](/wiki/Robert_Gates "Robert Gates"); 5th [Secretary of Homeland Security](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_Homeland_Security "United States Secretary of Homeland Security") and retired [Marine](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps") Gen. [John F. Kelly](/wiki/John_F._Kelly "John F. Kelly"); and 76th [Secretary of the Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Treasury "United States Secretary of the Treasury") [Jack Lew](/wiki/Jack_Lew "Jack Lew"). Other cabinet-level and senior executive branch officials include former [Director of National Intelligence](/wiki/Director_of_National_Intelligence "Director of National Intelligence") [Avril Haines](/wiki/Avril_Haines "Avril Haines"), former [CIA Director](/wiki/CIA_director "CIA director") [George Tenet](/wiki/George_Tenet "George Tenet"), 16th [Chair of the Federal Reserve](/wiki/Chair_of_the_Federal_Reserve "Chair of the Federal Reserve") [Jerome Powell](/wiki/Jerome_Powell "Jerome Powell"), [Director General of the Foreign Service](/wiki/Director_General_of_the_Foreign_Service "Director General of the Foreign Service") [Marcia Bernicat](/wiki/Marcia_Bernicat "Marcia Bernicat"), and seven [White House Chiefs of Staff](/wiki/White_House_Chief_of_Staff "White House Chief of Staff") that include [Ron Klain](/wiki/Ron_Klain "Ron Klain"), [Denis McDonough](/wiki/Denis_McDonough "Denis McDonough"), and [John Podesta](/wiki/John_Podesta "John Podesta").[[304]](#cite_note-313)[[305]](#cite_note-314) Former [President of the World Bank](/wiki/President_of_the_World_Bank_Group "President of the World Bank Group") [David Malpass](/wiki/David_Malpass "David Malpass") and former CIA Director Gen. [David Petraeus](/wiki/David_Petraeus "David Petraeus") are both affiliated with the School of Foreign Service, where they completed academic fellowships.[[306]](#cite_note-315)[[296]](#cite_note-:2-305) Notable non-graduate alumni include 36th President [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson") and the 13th and 21st Secretary of Defense [Donald Rumsfeld](/wiki/Donald_Rumsfeld "Donald Rumsfeld"), each of whom studied law at the [Georgetown University Law Center](/wiki/Georgetown_University_Law_Center "Georgetown University Law Center") but left without a degree.[[307]](#cite_note-316)[[308]](#cite_note-317)

In addition to high ranking diplomatic posts, Georgetown alumni have participated in significant historical events of the 20th century. Alumnus and longtime faculty member [Jan Karski](/wiki/Jan_Karski "Jan Karski") was a [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") resistance fighter and courier for the [Polish government-in-exile](/wiki/Polish_government-in-exile "Polish government-in-exile"), where he was among the first to [reveal the atrocities of the Holocaust to the world](/wiki/Karski%27s_reports "Karski's reports").[[309]](#cite_note-318) Alumnus, Jesuit, career diplomat and founder of the School of Foreign Service [Edmund A. Walsh](/wiki/Edmund_A._Walsh "Edmund A. Walsh") also played a role in the investigations of the [Nuremberg trials](/wiki/Nuremberg_trials "Nuremberg trials") and [Soviet war crimes](/wiki/Soviet_war_crimes "Soviet war crimes").[[310]](#cite_note-319)[[311]](#cite_note-320)

In the [119th U.S. Congress](/wiki/119th_United_States_Congress "119th United States Congress"), alumni [Hakeem Jeffries](/wiki/Hakeem_Jeffries "Hakeem Jeffries"), [John Barrasso](/wiki/John_Barrasso "John Barrasso"), and [Dick Durbin](/wiki/Dick_Durbin "Dick Durbin") hold party leadership positions, serving among a total of seven alumni in the [United States Senate](/wiki/List_of_current_United_States_senators "List of current United States senators") and 21 alumni and faculty in the [House of Representatives](/wiki/List_of_current_United_States_representatives "List of current United States representatives").[[312]](#cite_note-321) In total, 116 alumni have served in Congress and 26 have served as state governors, including [Terry McAuliffe](/wiki/Terry_McAuliffe "Terry McAuliffe") and [Pat Quinn](/wiki/Pat_Quinn_(politician) "Pat Quinn (politician)"). On the [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/United_States_Supreme_Court "United States Supreme Court"), alumni include the late Associate Justice [Antonin Scalia](/wiki/Antonin_Scalia "Antonin Scalia") and former Chief Justice [Edward Douglass White](/wiki/Edward_Douglass_White "Edward Douglass White").[[313]](#cite_note-322)[[314]](#cite_note-323) Alumnus and former [Solicitor General of the United States](/wiki/Solicitor_General_of_the_United_States "Solicitor General of the United States") [Paul Clement](/wiki/Paul_Clement "Paul Clement") remains affiliated with the university as a member of the faculties of law and government.[[118]](#cite_note-:1-125)

The Hoya Battalion, the school's [Army ROTC](/wiki/Army_Reserve_Officers%27_Training_Corps "Army Reserve Officers' Training Corps") program, was ranked as the best in the country in 2012 for preparing cadets for military service,[[315]](#cite_note-324) and its graduates, such as former [Chief of Staff of the United States Army](/wiki/Chief_of_Staff_of_the_United_States_Army "Chief of Staff of the United States Army") [George W. Casey Jr.](/wiki/George_W._Casey_Jr. "George W. Casey Jr."), have gone on to serve at every rank, including [four-star general](/wiki/General_(United_States) "General (United States)"). Georgetown alumni include at least 23 officers promoted to general or [flag rank](/wiki/Flag_officer "Flag officer") across the joint service, including [Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Joint_Chiefs_of_Staff "Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff") Gen. [Joseph Dunford](/wiki/Joseph_Dunford "Joseph Dunford") and former [NATO Supreme Allied Commander](/wiki/Supreme_Allied_Commander_Atlantic "Supreme Allied Commander Atlantic") Gen. [John J. Sheehan](/wiki/John_J._Sheehan "John J. Sheehan").[[316]](#cite_note-325)[[317]](#cite_note-326)[[318]](#cite_note-327)

Twelve foreign heads of state and government have graduated from Georgetown, including King [Felipe VI of Spain](/wiki/Felipe_VI_of_Spain "Felipe VI of Spain"), King [Abdullah II of Jordan](/wiki/Abdullah_II_of_Jordan "Abdullah II of Jordan")[[319]](#cite_note-eilers-328)[[320]](#cite_note-cvoa-329) presidents [Alfonso López Michelsen](/wiki/Alfonso_L%C3%B3pez_Michelsen "Alfonso López Michelsen") and [Iván Duque](/wiki/Iv%C3%A1n_Duque "Iván Duque") of [Colombia](/wiki/President_of_Colombia "President of Colombia"),[[321]](#cite_note-330)[[322]](#cite_note-331) [Galo Plaza](/wiki/Galo_Plaza "Galo Plaza") of [Ecuador](/wiki/President_of_Ecuador "President of Ecuador"),[[323]](#cite_note-332) [Ricardo Arias](/wiki/Ricardo_Arias_(politician) "Ricardo Arias (politician)") of [Panama](/wiki/President_of_Panama "President of Panama"),[[324]](#cite_note-333) [Alfredo Cristiani](/wiki/Alfredo_Cristiani "Alfredo Cristiani") of [El Salvador](/wiki/President_of_El_Salvador "President of El Salvador"),[[325]](#cite_note-334) [Laura Chinchilla](/wiki/Laura_Chinchilla "Laura Chinchilla") of [Costa Rica](/wiki/President_of_Costa_Rica "President of Costa Rica"),[[326]](#cite_note-335) [Gloria Macapagal-Arroyo](/wiki/Gloria_Macapagal_Arroyo "Gloria Macapagal Arroyo") of the [Philippines](/wiki/President_of_the_Philippines "President of the Philippines"),[[327]](#cite_note-336) [Prime Minister of Lebanon](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Lebanon "Prime Minister of Lebanon") [Saad Hariri](/wiki/Saad_Hariri "Saad Hariri"),[[328]](#cite_note-337) [Prime Minister of Portugal](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Portugal "Prime Minister of Portugal") and [President of the European Commission](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Commission "President of the European Commission") [José Manuel Barroso](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Manuel_Barroso "José Manuel Barroso"),[[329]](#cite_note-338) and member of the [Presidency of Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Presidency_of_Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Presidency of Bosnia and Herzegovina") [Željko Komšić](/wiki/%C5%BDeljko_Kom%C5%A1i%C4%87 "Željko Komšić").[[330]](#cite_note-339)

In business, Georgetown billionaire alumni include real estate developer and sports team owner [Frank McCourt](/wiki/Frank_McCourt_(executive) "Frank McCourt (executive)"), sports entrepreneur [Ted Leonsis](/wiki/Ted_Leonsis "Ted Leonsis"), venture capital investor [Chris Sacca](/wiki/Chris_Sacca "Chris Sacca"), [Ipsen](/wiki/Ipsen "Ipsen") heir [Henri Beaufour](/wiki/Henri_Beaufour "Henri Beaufour"), private equity investor [Antonio Gracias](/wiki/Antonio_Gracias "Antonio Gracias"), Swedish heir [Marcus Wallenberg](/wiki/Marcus_Wallenberg_(born_1956) "Marcus Wallenberg (born 1956)"), founder of [Kroll Inc.](/wiki/Kroll_Inc. "Kroll Inc.") [Jules Kroll](/wiki/Jules_Kroll "Jules Kroll"), Hong Kong financier [Eric Hotung](/wiki/Eric_Hotung "Eric Hotung"), and [MBNA](/wiki/MBNA "MBNA") founder [Charles Cawley](/wiki/Charles_Cawley "Charles Cawley").[[331]](#cite_note-340)[[332]](#cite_note-341)[[333]](#cite_note-342)[[334]](#cite_note-343)[[335]](#cite_note-344)[[336]](#cite_note-345)[[337]](#cite_note-346)[[338]](#cite_note-347) Other prominent alumni in business include [JPMorgan Chase](/wiki/JPMorgan_Chase "JPMorgan Chase") executive [Mary Callahan Erdoes](/wiki/Mary_Callahan_Erdoes "Mary Callahan Erdoes"), former [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros. "Warner Bros.") CEO [Ann Sarnoff](/wiki/Ann_Sarnoff "Ann Sarnoff"), investor [Paul Pelosi](/wiki/Paul_Pelosi "Paul Pelosi") and executives [Laurence Tosi](/wiki/Laurence_Tosi "Laurence Tosi"), [Gary Perlin](/wiki/Gary_Perlin "Gary Perlin"), [David Wehner](/wiki/David_Wehner "David Wehner"), [Patricia Russo](/wiki/Patricia_Russo "Patricia Russo"), and [Charles Prince](/wiki/Charles_Prince "Charles Prince").

Georgetown alumni in journalism have been awarded the [Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize"), [Edward R. Murrow Award](/wiki/Edward_R._Murrow_Award_(Radio_Television_Digital_News_Association) "Edward R. Murrow Award (Radio Television Digital News Association)"), and [Peabody Awards](/wiki/Peabody_Awards "Peabody Awards") for their reporting.[[339]](#cite_note-348)[[340]](#cite_note-349) Pulitzer Prize winners include [Walter Pincus](/wiki/Walter_Pincus "Walter Pincus"), [John Bersia](/wiki/John_Bersia "John Bersia"), and [Megan Twohey](/wiki/Megan_Twohey "Megan Twohey"). [Joan Biskupic](/wiki/Joan_Biskupic "Joan Biskupic") was a Pulitzer finalist for her coverage of the Supreme Court.[[341]](#cite_note-350)

Alumni in the arts include [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Adapted_Screenplay "Academy Award for Best Adapted Screenplay")-winning screenwriter and novelist [William Peter Blatty](/wiki/William_Peter_Blatty "William Peter Blatty"), whose novel [*The Exorcist*](/wiki/The_Exorcist_(novel) "The Exorcist (novel)") and its [film adaptation](/wiki/The_Exorcist "The Exorcist") are set on the Georgetown campus, where the film was [shot on location](/wiki/Location_shooting "Location shooting").[[342]](#cite_note-351) Filmmaker [Bradley Cooper](/wiki/Bradley_Cooper "Bradley Cooper") has been [nominated for 12 Academy Awards](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Bradley_Cooper "List of awards and nominations received by Bradley Cooper") as a producer, writer, and actor.[[343]](#cite_note-352) Screenwriter [Jonathan Nolan](/wiki/Jonathan_Nolan "Jonathan Nolan") is known for the films *[Interstellar](/wiki/Interstellar_(film) "Interstellar (film)")* and *[The Dark Knight](/wiki/The_Dark_Knight "The Dark Knight")*, as well as the [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Original_Screenplay "Academy Award for Best Original Screenplay")-nominated *[Memento](/wiki/Memento_(film) "Memento (film)")*.[[344]](#cite_note-353) Other Academy Award-nominated alumni include [Golden Globe](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Motion_Picture_%E2%80%93_Drama "Golden Globe Award for Best Motion Picture – Drama") winner [Andrew Morrison](/wiki/Andrew_Morrison_(filmmaker) "Andrew Morrison (filmmaker)") and writer-director [RaMell Ross](/wiki/RaMell_Ross "RaMell Ross").[[345]](#cite_note-354) [Tony Award](/wiki/Tony_Awards "Tony Awards")-winning alumni include [John Guare](/wiki/John_Guare "John Guare") and [Jack Hofsiss](/wiki/Jack_Hofsiss "Jack Hofsiss").[[346]](#cite_note-355)[[347]](#cite_note-356)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgetown_University&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** *Collegium Georgiopolitanum* is the Latin [nominative case](/wiki/Nominative_case "Nominative case"). On Georgetown diplomas, the name appears in the [genitive case](/wiki/Genitive_case "Genitive case") in the phrase *Praeses et Professores Collegii Georgiopolitani* (tr. "President and Faculty of Georgetown College").[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-motto_6-0)** *Utraque Unum* is [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") from Paul's [Epistle to the Ephesians](/wiki/Epistle_to_the_Ephesians "Epistle to the Ephesians") 2:14. See [official explanation](http://www.georgetown.edu/about/). [Other translations available](http://biblehub.com/ephesians/2-14.htm).
3. **[^](#cite_ref-13)** as [Georgetown College](/wiki/Georgetown_University_College_of_Arts_%26_Sciences "Georgetown University College of Arts & Sciences")
4. **[^](#cite_ref-14)** Predating the establishment of the [District of Columbia](/wiki/District_of_Columbia "District of Columbia"), which annexed the settlement of [Georgetown](/wiki/Georgetown_(Washington,_D.C.) "Georgetown (Washington, D.C.)"), then part of [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland"), in 1801.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-healy_30-0)** While [Patrick Francis Healy](/wiki/Patrick_Francis_Healy "Patrick Francis Healy") inherited African ancestry from his mother and was consequently classified as racially black according to the "[one-drop rule](/wiki/One-drop_rule "One-drop rule")" of 19th-century American society, he self-identified racially as white and ethnically as Irish American.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-77)** The School of Dentistry was founded in 1901 as a department of the School of Medicine, and was elevated to a school in 1951. The school was disestablished in 1990.[[70]](#cite_note-75)[[71]](#cite_note-76)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-81)** The School of Languages and Linguistics was created out of the School of Foreign Service in 1949 as the Institute of Languages and Linguistics. It was elevated to a school in 1959. In 1994, the school was subsumed primarily by the College of Arts & Sciences.[[72]](#cite_note-78)[[73]](#cite_note-79)[[74]](#cite_note-80)
8. **[^](#cite_ref-178)** Other consists of [Multiracial Americans](/wiki/Multiracial_Americans "Multiracial Americans") & those who prefer to not say.
9. **[^](#cite_ref-179)** The percentage of students who received an income-based federal [Pell grant](/wiki/Pell_grant "Pell grant") intended for low-income students.
